Question: Scenario-based: A woman writes a formal letter explaining how she wants her property to be divided after her death. Does this letter count as 'a will'?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The words "a will" denote any testamentary document.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the legal definition of 'a will' under the Penal Code?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The term 'a will' is defined as any document that is testamentary in nature.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the woman's letter provides instructions for the distribution of her estate after death, it constitutes a testamentary document and thus a will.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 31, the letter counts as a will because the law defines 'a will' as any testamentary document.

Question: Scenario-based: A new law is passed specifically to regulate the fishing industry in Bangladesh. What is the legal term for this type of law according to the Code?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A "special law" is a law applicable to a particular subject.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How does the Penal Code define a law that only applies to one specific subject matter?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Any law that is designed to apply to a particular subject is classified as a 'special law'.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the fishing regulation applies only to the specific subject of the fishing industry, it fits the statutory definition of a special law.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 41, this law is called a 'special law' because it applies to a particular subject.

Question: Scenario-based: I am accused of a crime that happened in Dhaka at midnight. Can I show the judge a video of me at a party in Sylhet at that same time?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–


    (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact;


    (2) If by themselves or in connection with other facts they make the existence or non-existence of any fact in issue or relevant fact highly probable or improbable.


    Illustrations:


    (a) The question is whether A committed a crime at Chittagong on a certain day.


    The fact that, on that day, A was at Dhaka is relevant.


    The fact that, near the time when the crime was committed, A was at a distance from the place where it was committed, which would render it highly improbable, though not impossible, that he committed it, is relevant.


    (b) The question is, whether A committed a crime.


    The circumstances are such that the crime must have been committed either by A, B, C or D. Every fact which shows that the crime could have been committed by no one else and that it was not committed by either B, C or D, is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an alibi admissible if it is inconsistent with the possibility of committing the crime?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Any fact inconsistent with a fact in issue, such as being in another city, is considered legally relevant.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Being in Sylhet is physically inconsistent with committing a crime in Dhaka at the same time; thus, Section 11(1) makes the party evidence relevant.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 11, Illustration (a), the video of you in Sylhet is relevant because it is inconsistent with you being in Dhaka.

Question: Scenario-based: A small wooden raft is used to move construction supplies across a river. Does this raft count as a 'vessel' under the law?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The word “vessel” denotes anything made for the conveyance by water of human beings or of property.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a simple raft classified as a 'vessel' if it is used to transport property on water?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A 'vessel' includes any object created for the purpose of carrying people or property through water.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the raft was made to convey construction supplies (property) across the river, it fulfills the legal requirements for a vessel.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 48, the raft is a vessel because it was made for the conveyance of property by water.

Question: Scenario-based: In a court dispute about damage caused by a pet bird, the owner argues that a bird is not an 'animal' because the word only refers to mammals like dogs or cats. Is he correct?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The word "animal" denotes any living creature, other than a human being.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the legal term 'animal' include non-mammals like birds?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The statutory definition of 'animal' is all-encompassing, covering every living creature except for human beings.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As a bird is a living creature and not human, it falls clearly within the broad definition of an animal provided by the Code.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 47, the owner is incorrect because the law defines 'animal' as any living creature other than a human being.

Question: Scenario-based: A legal dispute arises over whether a specific neighborhood in Dhaka counts as 'the public.' How does the law define this term?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The word 'public' includes any class of the public or any community.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the term 'public' apply to smaller segments like specific communities or classes?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The statutory definition of 'public' is inclusive of any particular community or class within the general population.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the neighborhood constitutes a community, it fits the legal definition of 'public' for the purposes of establishing any related legal rights or violations.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 12, the neighborhood counts as the public because the legal definition includes any class of the public or any community.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim is attacked by a robber and hits him back to save himself. Is Salim's act of hitting the robber considered a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an otherwise criminal act excused if performed while exercising the right of self-defense?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Any action taken within the legitimate boundaries of the right of private defense is exempt from being classified as an offense.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since Salim acted solely to defend himself from an attack, his conduct is legally justified and does not constitute a crime.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 96, Salim's act is not a crime because it was done in the exercise of his legal right of private defense.

Question: Scenario-based: A 7-year-old child picks up an expensive watch from a store and walks out with it. Can the store owner press criminal charges for theft against the child?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under nine years of age.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the law allow for the criminal prosecution of a child who is very young?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: No act performed by a child who has not yet reached the age of nine is considered a criminal offense.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the child is only seven years old, they fall below the mandatory age threshold, making it impossible to charge them.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 82, the child cannot be charged with theft because the law states that nothing done by a child under nine years of age is considered a criminal offense.

Question: Scenario-based: Someone wants to know if the rules of the Limitation Act, 1908 apply in every city and village across the entire country of Bangladesh. Is the Act limited to certain areas?

Possible Sections.Full Text: (1) This Act may be called the Limitation Act, 1908. (2) It extends to the whole of Bangladesh. (3) This section and section 31 shall come into force at once. The rest of this Act shall come into force on the first day of January, 1909.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the territorial extent of the Limitation Act, 1908?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The Act is a national law that applies uniformly across the entire geographic territory of the country.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Under Section 1(2), the Act's jurisdiction encompasses all regions of Bangladesh. There are no geographic exclusions; the limitation rules apply to any suit filed within the nation's borders.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 1, Subsection (2), the law applies to the whole of Bangladesh — it covers every part of the country without any exceptions.

Question: Scenario-based: A woman is arrested for a very serious crime that usually doesn't allow for bail. Does the judge have the power to still give her bail because of her gender?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 497(1)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) When any person accused of any non-bailable offence is arrested or detained without warrant by an officer in charge of a police-station, or appears or is brought before a Court, he may be released on bail, but he shall not be so released if there appear reasonable grounds for believing that he has been guilty of an offence punishable with death or transportation for life:


    Provided that the Court may direct that any person under the age of sixteen years or any woman or any sick or infirm person accused of such an offence be released on bail.


    (2) If it appears to such officer or Court at any stage of the investigation, inquiry or trial, as the case may be, that there are not reasonable grounds for believing that the accused has committed a non-bailable offence, but that there are sufficient grounds for further inquiry into his guilt, the accused shall, pending such inquiry, be released on bail, or, at the discretion of such officer or Court, on the execution by him of a bond without sureties for his appearance as hereinafter provided.


    (3) An officer or a Court releasing any person on bail under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2) shall record in writing his or its reasons for so doing.


    (4) If, at any time after the conclusion of the trial of a person accused of a non-bailable offence and before judgment is delivered, the Court is of opinion that there are reasonable grounds for believing that the accused is not guilty of any such offence, it shall release the accused, if he is in custody on the execution by him of a bond without sureties for his appearance to hear judgment delivered.


    (5) The High Court Division or Court of Session and, in the case of a person released by itself, any other Court may cause any person who has been released under this section to be arrested and may commit him to custody.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 497(1)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a court grant bail to a woman accused of a non-bailable capital offence?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: While bail is generally denied for death-penalty crimes, a proviso allows the court to grant bail to women, children under sixteen, or sick persons.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Under the proviso to Section 497(1), the judge has the discretionary power to release the woman on bail despite the seriousness of the crime, based on her gender.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 497, Subsection (1), the judge can choose to give bail to a woman even for a very serious crime that is normally non-bailable.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 497(1)

Question: Scenario-based: In a legal case involving a crime committed by a 5-year-old girl, the defense argues that the term 'woman' does not apply to a child. Is this correct under the law?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The word 'man' denotes a male human being of any age: the word 'woman' denotes a female human being of any age.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the legal term 'woman' include female human beings regardless of their age?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Statutory definitions specify that 'man' and 'woman' represent male and female human beings of any age group.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the five-year-old child is a female human being, she falls directly under the definition of 'woman' provided in the Code.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 10, the defense is incorrect because the word 'woman' legally denotes a female human being of any age, including a child.

Question: Scenario-based: I am accused of robbing a store in Dhaka at 3:00 PM. I can prove I was signing a legal document in Chittagong at 2:55 PM. Is this evidence allowed in my trial?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–


    (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact;


    (2) If by themselves or in connection with other facts they make the existence or non-existence of any fact in issue or relevant fact highly probable or improbable.


    Illustrations:


    (a) The question is whether A committed a crime at Chittagong on a certain day.


    The fact that, on that day, A was at Dhaka is relevant.


    The fact that, near the time when the crime was committed, A was at a distance from the place where it was committed, which would render it highly improbable, though not impossible, that he committed it, is relevant.


    (b) The question is, whether A committed a crime.


    The circumstances are such that the crime must have been committed either by A, B, C or D. Every fact which shows that the crime could have been committed by no one else and that it was not committed by either B, C or D, is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an alibi relevant if it is physically inconsistent with the possibility of the accused committing the crime?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Any fact that is inconsistent with a fact in issue (like a person's presence at a crime) is considered legally relevant.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Being in Chittagong at 2:55 PM is physically inconsistent with a robbery in Dhaka at 3:00 PM; therefore, Section 11(1) makes the document signing relevant.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 11, Illustration (a), yes. Your evidence is relevant because it is inconsistent with the fact that you committed the robbery in a different city.

Question: My family has used a specific path to the river for fifty years. Can I use an old land sale document from my grandfather to prove this right?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–


(a) any transaction by which the right or custom in question was created, claimed, modified, recognized, asserted or denied, or which was inconsistent with its existence;


(b) particular instances in which the right or custom was claimed, recognized or exercised, or in which its exercise was disputed, asserted or departed from.


Illustration


The question is whether A has a right to a fishery. A deed conferring the fishery on A's ancestors, a mortgage of the fishery by A's father, a subsequent grant of the fishery by A's father, irreconcilable with the mortgage, particular instances in which A's father exercised the right, or in which the exercise of the right was stopped by A's neighbours, are relevant facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a historical transaction document relevant to proving the existence of a right?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: When the existence of a right is questioned, any transaction by which that right was claimed or recognized is relevant.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The grandfather's land document is a transaction where the family's right was recognized or claimed, making it a relevant fact under Section 13(a).
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 13, Clause (a), the old document is relevant because it is a transaction where your family's right was claimed or recognized.

Question: Scenario-based: A man lies about his business partner to ruin the partner's reputation. Does this harm to reputation count as an 'injury' in the legal sense?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The word "injury" denotes any harm whatever illegally caused to any person, in body, mind, reputation or property.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is the definition of 'injury' limited only to physical bodily harm?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Legal 'injury' encompasses any illegal harm caused to a person's body, mind, property, or reputation.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the partner's reputation was illegally harmed, the act falls squarely within the broad statutory definition of an injury.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 44, harming the partner's reputation is an injury because the law defines injury as harm caused to a person's body, mind, property, or reputation.

Question: Scenario-based: I was arrested and confessed to everything while talking to the Sub-Inspector at the station. Can that confession be used to prove my guilt in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: No confession made to a police-officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a confession made directly to a police officer be proved against an accused person?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The Act imposes an absolute bar on the use of confessions made to police officers as evidence against an accused person.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the client confessed directly to a police Sub-Inspector, Section 25 applies strictly, prohibiting the prosecutor from proving that statement as evidence.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 25, no confession you make to a police officer can be proved against you in a court of law.

Question: Scenario-based: Kamal signs a document using a different name just for a joke among friends, with no intent to cheat anyone or gain money. Has he acted 'fraudulently'?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A person is said to do a thing fraudulently if he does that thing with intent to defraud but not otherwise.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an act considered fraudulent if it lacks the specific intent to defraud?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: An action is only classified as fraudulent if the person performs it with a clear intent to deceive or defraud.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since Kamal acted for amusement without any intent to cause harm or gain, his behavior does not meet the requirement for being 'fraudulent'.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 25, Kamal did not act fraudulently because the law requires a specific intent to defraud for an act to be called fraudulent.

Question: If a crime could only have been committed by one of four specific people, and I prove that the other three were locked in a room at the time, is that proof relevant to my trial?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Facts not otherwise relevant are relevant–


    (1) If they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact;


    (2) If by themselves or in connection with other facts they make the existence or non-existence of any fact in issue or relevant fact highly probable or improbable.


    Illustrations:


    (a) The question is whether A committed a crime at Chittagong on a certain day.


    The fact that, on that day, A was at Dhaka is relevant.


    The fact that, near the time when the crime was committed, A was at a distance from the place where it was committed, which would render it highly improbable, though not impossible, that he committed it, is relevant.


    (b) The question is, whether A committed a crime.


    The circumstances are such that the crime must have been committed either by A, B, C or D. Every fact which shows that the crime could have been committed by no one else and that it was not committed by either B, C or D, is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a fact that excludes other suspects relevant to proving the liability of a remaining suspect?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Facts are relevant if they make the existence of a fact in issue (like a person's guilt) highly probable or improbable.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: By proving the other three candidates were elsewhere, the guilt of the fourth person becomes highly probable, triggering relevancy under Section 11(2), Illustration (b).
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 11, Illustration (b), the fact that the other three people were elsewhere is relevant because it makes it highly probable that you committed the crime.

Question: If a village claims they have a traditional right to walk across a private field, can they show instances where their ancestors were stopped from walking there to prove the right exists?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–


(a) any transaction by which the right or custom in question was created, claimed, modified, recognized, asserted or denied, or which was inconsistent with its existence;


(b) particular instances in which the right or custom was claimed, recognized or exercised, or in which its exercise was disputed, asserted or departed from.


Illustration


The question is whether A has a right to a fishery. A deed conferring the fishery on A's ancestors, a mortgage of the fishery by A's father, a subsequent grant of the fishery by A's father, irreconcilable with the mortgage, particular instances in which A's father exercised the right, or in which the exercise of the right was stopped by A's neighbours, are relevant facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are instances where a right was disputed or stopped relevant to proving its existence?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: When a right is in question, any instance where that right was claimed, disputed, or exercised is legally relevant.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The village being stopped is an instance where the right was 'disputed' or 'asserted,' which is specifically made relevant by Section 13(b).
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 13, Clause (b), instances where the exercise of the right was disputed or stopped are relevant to deciding if the right exists.

Question: Scenario-based: A man is found guilty of intentionally killing his neighbor. What are the two main types of prison or capital punishments the judge can choose to give him?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What are the statutory penalties prescribed for the offense of murder?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The legal punishment for intentionally causing death is limited to either the death penalty or a sentence of life imprisonment.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the conviction is for murder, the court must select between the two specified maximum penalties while also potentially imposing a fine.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 302, the judge can punish the man with either death or imprisonment for life, and the man will also be liable to pay a fine.

Question: Scenario-based: Two men get into a heated physical fight in the middle of a busy public park, scaring everyone around them. Have they committed the specific crime of 'affray'?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When two or more persons, by fighting in a public place, disturb the public peace, they are said to "commit an affray.”

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What elements must be present for a physical altercation to be legally classified as an affray?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: An affray occurs when at least two individuals fight in a public area and consequently disrupt the peace of the public.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the fight involved two men and took place in a public park, disturbing those present, the criteria for an affray are met.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 159, the men have committed an affray because they were fighting in a public place and disturbing the public peace.

Question: Scenario-based: A husband and wife want to use the rules in this Act for their divorce case. Does the Limitation Act apply to cases filed under the Divorce Act?

Possible Sections.Full Text: (1) Nothing in this Act shall affect section 25 of the Contract Act, 1872. (2) Where any special law prescribes for any suit, appeal or application a period of limitation different from the period prescribed therefore by the first schedule, the provision of section 3 shall apply, as if such period were prescribed therefor in that schedule, and for the purpose of determining any period of limitation prescribed for any suit, appeal or application by any special law- (a) the provisions contained in section 4, sections 9 to 18, and section 22 shall apply only in so far as, and to the extent to which, they are not expressly excluded by such special law; and (b) the remaining provisions of this Act shall not apply. (3) Nothing in this Act shall apply to suits under the Divorce Act. (4) Sections 26 and 27 and the definition of "easement" in section 2 shall not apply to cases arising in territories to which the Easements Act, 1882, may for the time being extend.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are the limitation periods and rules of the Limitation Act, 1908 applicable to proceedings under the Divorce Act?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The Limitation Act specifically excludes all suits and proceedings initiated under the Divorce Act from its scope.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Under Section 29(3), the rules of the Limitation Act do not govern divorce cases. The couple must look to the Divorce Act itself for any relevant time limits or procedural rules.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 29, Subsection (3), this Act does not apply to any cases filed under the Divorce Act — divorce cases are entirely separate from these rules.

Question: Scenario-based: A man is accused of a crime that involved hitting someone five times. Does the law treat these five hits as a single 'act' or five separate acts?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The word "act" denotes as well a series of acts as a single act: the word "omission" denotes as well a series of omissions as a single omission.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the term 'act' include multiple actions or only a single physical movement?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: In legal terminology, the word 'act' encompasses both a single action and a continuous series of actions.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The five hits constitute a series of acts, which the Code allows to be unified under the singular definition of an 'act'.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 33, the five hits can be treated as a single act because the law defines 'act' to include a series of acts.

Question: Scenario-based: Kamal is in jail because he couldn't pay his fine. After two months, his family finally pays the full fine to the authorities. Must the jail release him immediately?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The imprisonment which is imposed in default of payment of a fine shall terminate whenever that fine is either paid or levied by process of law.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the payment of a fine end the imprisonment imposed for its non-payment?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Imprisonment served in default of a fine must terminate the moment the fine is fully paid or legally recovered.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the fine has now been paid in full, Kamal's additional prison term must end immediately according to the statutory rule.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 68, the imprisonment must terminate immediately because the fine has been paid in full.

Question: Scenario-based: Five people in an unlawful assembly start using physical force to push people out of their way. What is the maximum sentence for this act of rioting?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever is guilty of rioting, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the statutory punishment for the offense of rioting?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The act of rioting (using force in an unlawful assembly) is punishable by a maximum term of two years in prison or a fine.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the group used physical force in pursuit of their common goal, they are guilty of rioting under Section 147, allowing for a two-year sentence.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 147, the maximum punishment for rioting is two years in prison, a fine, or both.

Question: Scenario-based: A court issues an order to prevent a neighbor from cutting down a boundary tree that is part of a dispute. What is the specific legal term for this type of relief?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Specific relief granted under clause (c) of section 5 is called preventive relief.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the legal designation for relief intended to prevent a party from performing an act they are obligated not to do?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Relief that prevents a party from doing an act they shouldn't do is formally termed 'preventive relief.'
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: By stopping the neighbor from cutting the tree, the court is granting relief under Section 5(c), which Section 6 defines as preventive relief.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 6, this type of help is called 'preventive relief' because it stops a person from doing something they shouldn't do.

Question: Scenario-based: A wants to get an injunction to stop his neighbor from building a fence on his land. Is the court required to give him one if he proves he is right?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Preventive relief is granted at the discretion of the Court by injunction, temporary or perpetual.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is the granting of an injunction a mandatory duty of the court or a matter of discretion?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Granting preventive relief through an injunction is a discretionary power of the court, not a mandatory obligation.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Section 52 states that the court uses its 'discretion' to decide whether or not to grant an injunction, meaning it's not guaranteed even if A is right.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 52, the court has the choice (discretion) to grant an injunction; it is not automatically required to do so.

Question: Scenario-based: A court in Dhaka issues a warrant for a man who has fled to a remote village in Chittagong. Is the police officer allowed to travel there to make the arrest?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 82
Possible Sections.Full Text: A warrant of arrest may be executed at any place in Bangladesh.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 82

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the territorial scope for the execution of a judicial arrest warrant in Bangladesh?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The law permits an arrest warrant to be carried out and enforced at any geographic location within the national borders.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The officer possesses the legal authority to execute the Dhaka warrant in Chittagong, as Section 82 grants nationwide reach to all valid arrest warrants.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 82, the police officer is allowed to make the arrest in Chittagong because a warrant can be used anywhere within Bangladesh.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 82

Question: Scenario-based: A legal document mentions the term 'Good Faith' regarding a property transfer. According to this specific Act, what is the simple test to see if someone acted in good faith?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In this Act, unless there is anything repugnant in the subject or context,- (1) "applicant" includes any person from or through whom an applicant derives his right to apply: (2) "bill of exchange" includes a hundi and a cheque: (3) "bond" includes any instrument whereby a person obliges himself to pay money to another, on condition that the obligation shall be void if a specified act is performed, or is not performed, as the case may be: (4) "defendant" includes any person from or through whom a defendant derives his liability to be sued: (5) "easement" includes a right not arising from contract, by which one person is entitled to remove and appropriate for his own profit any part of the soil belonging to another or anything growing in, or attached to or subsisting upon, the land of another: (6) "foreign country" means any country other than Bangladesh: (7) "good faith": nothing shall be deemed to be done in good faith which is not done with due care and attention: (8) "plaintiff" includes any person from or through whom a plaintiff derives his right to sue: (9) "promissory note" means any instrument whereby the maker engages absolutely to pay a specified sum of money to another at a time therein limited, or on demand, or at sight: (10) "suit" does not include an appeal or application: and (11) "trustee" does not include a benamider, a mortgagee remaining in possession after the mortgage has been satisfied, or a wrong-doer in possession without title.
Part II
LIMITATION OF SUITS, APPEALS AND APPLICATIONS

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the statutory definition of 'good faith' under the Limitation Act?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: An act is only considered to be in good faith if it is performed with proper care and attention.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: To prove good faith under Section 2(7), it is not enough to be honest; the person must demonstrate they acted with 'due care and attention' during the transaction or legal process.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 2, Subsection (7), an act is only considered to be in 'good faith' if it was done with proper care and attention.

Question: Scenario-based: A man helps his friend commit a theft. Since the friend was caught and the theft was actually completed, how is the helper's punishment decided?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 109
Possible Sections.Full Text:
Whenever District Magistrate or an Executive Magistrate receive information-


    (a) that any person is taking precautions to conceal his presence within the local limits of such Magistrate's jurisdiction, and that there is reason to believe that such person is taking such precautions with a view to committing any offence, or


    (b) that there is within such limits a person who has no ostensible means of subsistence, or who cannot give a satisfactory account of himself,


    such Magistrate may, in manner hereinafter provided, require such person to show cause why he should not be ordered to execute a bond, with sureties, for his good behaviour for such period, not exceeding one year, as the Magistrate thinks fit to fix.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 109

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How does the law determine the penalty for an abettor when the intended crime is successfully carried out?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: An abettor is liable to the exact same punishment that the law provides for the primary offense they helped commit.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: According to CrPC Schedule 2, Section 109, the helper's sentence is not independent; it matches the theft penalty because the crime was finished in consequence of the help.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 109, the helper will receive the exact same punishment as the person who committed the theft.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 109
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A local newspaper published a false and damaging story about a businessman. From the day that story was printed and sold, how long does the businessman have to sue for libel?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 24
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for libel. One year.. When the libel is published.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 24

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period and trigger event for a libel compensation lawsuit?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A suit for libel must be initiated within one year, starting from the date the defamatory material is published.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The publication occurred the day the newspaper was printed and sold. Under Article 24, the businessman's one-year limit to seek legal compensation begins on that specific date of publication.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 24, the businessman has exactly one year to file his lawsuit — the time starts from the day the libelous story was published.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 24

Question: Scenario-based: A contract is signed on a specific date, and a legal deadline is set for 'one year' later. Which calendar must be used to figure out exactly when that year ends?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Wherever the word "year" or the word "month" is used, it is to be understood that the year or the month is to be reckoned according to the British calendar.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which calendar system is mandatory for calculating years and months in legal contexts?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The law requires that any mention of a 'year' or 'month' be calculated based on the British calendar.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: To determine the deadline, the court must follow the British calendar as mandated by Section 49, regardless of any other local calendar.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 49, the deadline must be calculated according to the British calendar for both years and months.

Question: Scenario-based: A man's wife is holding a bag of stolen goods for him in their home. Does the law consider the man to be in possession of those goods?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
When property is in the possession of a person's wife, clerk or servant, on account of that person, it is in that person's possession within the meaning of this Code.


Explanation.- A person employed temporarily or on a particular occasion in the capacity of a clerk, or servant, is a clerk or servant within the meaning of this section.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is property held by a wife legally considered to be in the husband's possession?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Property held by a spouse, clerk, or servant on behalf of a person is legally deemed to be in that person's possession.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the wife is holding the goods for the man, the legal fiction of possession applies, making the man responsible for the goods.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 27, the man is considered to be in possession of the goods because they are held by his wife on his account.

Question: Scenario-based: A judge in Sylhet issues a warrant to arrest a person. If that person travels to Comilla, can the police officer still arrest him there using the same paper?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 82
Possible Sections.Full Text: A warrant of arrest may be executed at any place in Bangladesh.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 82

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the territorial limit for the enforcement of an arrest warrant issued by a court in Bangladesh?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A valid arrest warrant carries authority that extends to every geographic location within the national borders of Bangladesh.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The officer is legally allowed to execute the Sylhet warrant in Comilla because Section 82 grants nationwide validity to all judicial arrest warrants in the country.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 82, the police officer can arrest the person in Comilla because a warrant can be used anywhere in Bangladesh.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 82
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A company is involved in a property dispute in Sylhet. Can they rely on the Specific Relief Act, 1877, to resolve this matter within the courts of Bangladesh?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Short title: This Act may be called the Specific Relief Act, 1877.


    Local Extent: It extends to the whole of Bangladesh.


    Commencement: And it shall come into force on the first day of May, 1877.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the Specific Relief Act, 1877, apply to all territorial regions within Bangladesh?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The Act's jurisdiction is defined as extending to the entire territory of Bangladesh.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the dispute is in Sylhet, which is part of Bangladesh, Section 1 confirms the Act is legally applicable to this scenario.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 1, the company can rely on this law because it explicitly extends to the whole of Bangladesh.

Question: Scenario-based: A doctor performs a dangerous surgery without checking the patient's medical history. He claims he acted in 'good faith' for the patient's benefit. Does the law support his claim?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is said to be done or believed in "good faith" which is done or believed without due care and attention.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does acting without proper care or attention prevent a person from claiming the defense of 'good faith'?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Legal 'good faith' is strictly defined as an act performed with the necessary degree of due care and professional attention.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The doctor's failure to check medical records shows a lack of due care, which legally disqualifies his actions from being considered 'good faith'.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 52, the doctor's claim is unsupported because the law states that nothing is done in good faith if it is done without due care and attention.

Question: Scenario-based: Two men are caught fighting in public and disturbing the peace. What is the longest time they can be sentenced to jail for this specific act?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever commits an affray shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to one hundred taka, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the maximum statutory imprisonment for the offense of affray?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The punishment for engaging in a public fight that disturbs the peace is limited to one month of imprisonment or a small fine.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the men committed an affray, the court's sentencing power is capped at the one-month limit set by Section 160.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 160, the maximum prison sentence the men can receive for committing an affray is one month.

Question: Scenario-based: Two men get into a heated argument and start punchng each other in the middle of a crowded train station, causing everyone nearby to run away in fear. Have they legally committed an 'affray'?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When two or more persons, by fighting in a public place, disturb the public peace, they are said to "commit an affray.”

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What elements define the crime of 'affray' when two people fight in a public area?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: An affray is established when two or more persons engage in physical combat in a public place and consequently disrupt the peace of the public.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the two men fought in a train station (a public place) and caused fear among the public, their conduct satisfies the statutory definition.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 159, the men have committed an affray because they were fighting in a public place and their actions disturbed the public peace.

Question: Scenario-based: After a case is officially filed, what is the next step the court takes to tell the defendant they need to come and answer the claim?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 27
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where a suit has been duly instituted, a summons may be issued to the defendant to appear and answer the claim and may be served in manner prescribed.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 27

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the formal mechanism for notifying a defendant of a pending lawsuit after it is instituted?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Once a case is filed, the court issues a summons ordering the defendant to appear and respond.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The institution of the suit triggers the court's duty under Section 27 to send an official summons to the person being sued.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 27, the court must issue a summons to the defendant, which is an official notice to come and answer the case.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 27
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A man is convicted of an offense where the law allows a fine but does not specify a maximum amount. Can the judge fine him a huge amount that exceeds his total wealth?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Where no sum is expressed to which a fine may extend, the amount of fine to which the offender is liable is unlimited, but shall not be excessive.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is there a limit to a fine when the specific statutory provision does not define a maximum sum?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: When no maximum fine is specified, the court may impose an unlimited amount, provided it is not disproportionately high.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: While the judge has broad power to determine the amount, Section 63 restricts this authority by forbidding excessive fines.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 63, the judge can set an unlimited fine since no sum is expressed, but the law strictly mandates that the amount must not be excessive for the offender.

Question: Scenario-based: A person was wrongly kept in a private room against their will for three days. After they were finally let out, how much time does the law give them to sue for compensation?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 19
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for false imprisonment. One year.. When the imprisonment ends.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 19

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period and starting trigger for a compensation claim regarding false imprisonment?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Victims of unlawful restraint have one year to initiate a lawsuit, with the period commencing immediately upon their release.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The countdown for the one-year limit begins the moment the person's confinement ends. Filing must occur within 365 days of that release to be legally valid under Article 19.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 19, the person has one year to file a suit for compensation — the time begins to run from the moment their imprisonment ends.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 19

Question: Scenario-based: A local manufacturing company is accused of a crime. The company's lawyer argues that only a human being can be a 'person' under the law. Is this argument legally sound?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The word 'person' includes any Company or Association, or body of persons, whether incorporated or not.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the legal term 'person' encompass corporate entities and associations in addition to natural human beings?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Statutory interpretation includes artificial legal entities like companies or groups within the definition, whether they are formally registered or not.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The manufacturing company qualifies as a 'body of persons' under the Code, making it a person for establishing legal liability.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 11, the company's argument is incorrect because the legal definition of a person specifically includes any company, association, or body of individuals, whether incorporated or not.

Question: Scenario-based: On the day of the hearing, the judge calls the case, but neither the plaintiff nor the defendant has showed up. What action can the court take regarding the suit?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where neither party appears when the suit is called on for hearing, the Court may make an order that the suit be dismissed.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 3

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the court's authority when both sides are absent at the scheduled time of the hearing?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The court has the power to dismiss a lawsuit if both the person suing and the person being sued fail to appear.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because both parties are missing when the case is called, Rule 3 grants the judge the discretion to dismiss the suit entirely.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 3, the court can dismiss the case if neither the plaintiff nor the defendant shows up for the hearing.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 3
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A domestic helper finished her work and left her job last month. Her employer still hasn't paid her final month's wages. How long does the law give her to sue for that unpaid salary?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 7
Possible Sections.Full Text: For the wages of a household servant, artisan or labourer. One year.. When the wages accrue due.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 7

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a household worker to recover unpaid wages?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A suit to recover salary for domestic service must be instituted within one year of the date the wages were supposed to be paid.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The helper's wages became due at the end of last month. Under Article 7, she has a one-year window from that accrual date to file a lawsuit against her employer for the unpaid amount.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 7, the helper has exactly one year to sue for her unpaid wages, counting from the day the salary was originally due.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 7

Question: When exactly did this specific set of criminal court rules start being used, and what is the official title I should use when referring to it?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 1
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) This Act may be called the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898; and it shall come into force on the first day of July, 1898.


    (2) It extends to the whole of Bangladesh; but, in the absence of any specific provision to the contrary, nothing herein contained shall affect any special law now in force, or any special jurisdiction or power conferred, or any special form of procedure prescribed, by any other law for the time being in force.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 1

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the formal designation and effective commencement date of the criminal procedure legislation?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The statute is identified as the 1898 procedural code and became operational on the first of July that same year.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Section 1 explicitly names the enactment and mandates its enforcement from July 1, 1898, establishing both its nomenclature and legal birthdate.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 1, Subsection (1), this law is titled the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, and it officially came into force on July 1, 1898.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 1
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A police officer is arresting a man in his home. Who must witness and sign the formal 'memorandum of arrest' to ensure the procedure is done correctly?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 46A(c)

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 46A(c)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What are the witness requirements for attesting a formal memorandum of arrest during a police operation?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The arrest memorandum must be attested by at least one witness who is either a family member or a reputable local resident.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: To comply with Section 46A(c)(i), the officer must secure the signature of either the man's relative or a respected neighbor to validate the arrest documentation.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 46A, Clause (c), Subclause (i), the memorandum of arrest must be witnessed by at least one family member or a respectable local resident.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 46A(c)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: Salim and Jamil get into a sudden physical fight in a busy public market. Their shouting and violence scare the shoppers and disturb the peace. What is this act called in legal terms?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When two or more persons, by fighting in a public place, disturb the public peace, they are said to "commit an affray.”

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How does the law define a physical fight between two people in a public location that disrupts the peace?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A public disturbance involving physical combat between two or more individuals is legally classified as a specific offense against public tranquility.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The fight in the public market between Salim and Jamil disturbed the peace, fulfilling the specific criteria of Section 159 to be designated an affray.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 159, this act is legally called an affray because it involved two people fighting in a public place and disturbing the peace.

Question: Scenario-based: A judge has finished hearing all witnesses and arguments in a civil trial. What is the very next step the judge must take, and what official document must follow that step?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 33
Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court, after the case has been heard, shall pronounce judgment, and on such judgment a decree shall follow.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 33

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the mandatory procedural sequence for concluding a civil trial after the final hearing?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The judge is required to first announce the formal judgment, which must then be followed by the issuance of a decree.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: After the hearing concludes, Section 33 dictates that the judge must state the grounds for the decision before the formal enforcement order is created.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 33, the judge must first announce the judgment in court, and then an official decree must be created based on that judgment.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 33
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A person wants to start a formal lawsuit. Does the law require them to prove the facts in their initial papers using a specific type of sworn document?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 26(2)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
24[1] Every suit shall be instituted by the presentation of a plaint or in such other manner as may be prescribed


25[(2) In every plaint, facts shall be proved by affidavit.]

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 26(2)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the mandatory method for verifying facts when initiating a civil lawsuit?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: All facts presented in the initial court filing must be supported and proved by an affidavit.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: To comply with Section 26(2), the plaintiff must attach a sworn affidavit to their plaint to confirm the truth of the allegations.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 26, Subsection (2), the person must prove the facts in their lawsuit papers by using a sworn affidavit.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 26(2)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A doctor honestly tells his patient that he will likely die in a few days. The patient is so shocked that he has a heart attack and dies immediately. Is the doctor guilty of a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
No communication made in good faith is an offence by reason of any harm to the person to whom it is made, if it is made for the benefit of that person.


Illustration:
A, a surgeon, in good faith, communicates to a patient his opinion that he cannot live. The patient dies in consequence of the shock. A has committed no offence, though he knew it to be likely that the communication might cause the patient's death.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person liable for harm caused by a truthful communication made in good faith for another's benefit?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Communications made in good faith for the recipient's benefit are exempt from criminal liability, even if they result in harm.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The doctor's honest assessment was intended for the patient's benefit; therefore, the fatal shock is protected under the Section 93 exception.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 93, the doctor is not guilty because his honest communication was made in good faith for the patient's benefit.

Question: I admitted to a debt in an email, but now I realize I was wrong. Can the other person use my email to stop me from changing my story in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but they may operate as estoppels under the provisions hereinafter contained.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can an admission function as a legal bar to prevent a party from contradicting their previous statement?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: While admissions aren't final proof, they can legally function as estoppels that prevent a person from later denying the truth of their admission.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The email admission is not conclusive proof, but Section 31 allows it to 'operate as an estoppel,' potentially barring the client from changing their story.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 31, while your admission isn't final proof, it can act as an 'estoppel,' which may prevent you from legally taking back what you said.

Question: I need to prove I mailed a check to my landlord. Can I use the fact that I put it in the office's outgoing mail tray as evidence?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.


Illustrations:


(a) The question is, whether a particular letter was dispatched.


The facts that it was the ordinary course of business for all letters put in a certain place to be carried to the post, and that that particular letter was put in that place are relevant.


(b) The question is, whether particular letter reached A.


The facts that it was posted in due course, and was not returned through the Dead Letter office, are relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a regular business practice relevant to proving that a specific act was performed?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The existence of a standard 'course of business' is relevant to proving that an act within that course was completed.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Placing the check in the tray is part of the 'course of business' for mailing, making that fact relevant under the first paragraph of Section 16.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 16, Illustration (a), the fact that it was the ordinary course of business to post items from that tray is a relevant fact.

Question: Scenario-based: Salma acted as a guarantor for a friend's loan. Her friend disappeared, so Salma had to pay the bank the full amount today. How long does she have to sue her friend to get her money back?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 81
Possible Sections.Full Text: By a surety against the principal debtor. Three years.. When the surety pays the creditor.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 81

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a surety (guarantor) to sue the principal debtor for reimbursement?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A surety has three years to file a suit for reimbursement, with the period commencing immediately upon payment to the creditor.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Salma paid the bank today. Under Article 81, she has a three-year window starting from this specific payment date to sue her friend for the money she spent as a guarantor.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 81, Salma has three years to sue her friend — the time limit starts today, the day she actually paid the bank.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 81

Question: Scenario-based: A judge sentences a man to prison. It later turns out the judge made a mistake about a small detail of the law. Can the man sue the judge personally for the error?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a Judge when acting judicially in the exercise of any power which is, or which in good faith he believes to be, given to him by law.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a judge personally liable for errors committed while acting judicially and in good faith?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Judges are exempt from criminal liability for any act performed while exercising judicial power they believe they possess in good faith.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the judge was acting in his official capacity and believed his actions were lawful, he is protected by judicial immunity.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 77, the man cannot sue the judge because a judge is not liable for acts done while acting judicially in good faith.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim is legally entitled to a plot of land but someone else is occupying it. To get it back, what legal procedure must Rahim follow according to this Act?

Possible Sections.Full Text: A person entitled to the possession of specific immoveable property may recover it in the manner prescribed by the Code of Civil Procedure.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the mandatory procedural path for recovering specific immoveable property under the Act?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Recovery of land or buildings must be conducted following the rules set by the Code of Civil Procedure.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Rahim, being entitled to possession, must initiate his recovery suit in the specific manner dictated by the Code of Civil Procedure as required by Section 8.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 8, Rahim must follow the procedures laid out in the Code of Civil Procedure to recover his land.

Question: Scenario-based: If the law states the court 'shall presume' a fact is true, what does the other side have to do to stop the judge from believing it?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Whenever it is provided by this Act that the Court may presume a fact, it may either regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved, or may call for proof of it.


"Shall presume" Whenever it is directed by this Act that the Court shall presume a fact, it shall regard such fact as proved, unless and until it is disproved.


"Conclusive proof" When one fact is declared by this Act to be conclusive proof of another, the Court shall, on proof of the one fact, regard the other as proved, and shall not allow evidence to be given for the purpose of disproving it.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: How can a mandatory presumption under 'shall presume' be rebutted by an opposing party?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A 'shall presume' directive forces the court to accept a fact as proved unless the opponent provides enough evidence to disprove it.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: To overcome the presumption, the other side must actively disprove the fact; otherwise, Section 4 mandates that the judge regard the fact as fully proved.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 4, the other side must provide evidence to 'disprove' the fact. Until they do, the judge must accept the fact as proved.

Question: Scenario-based: A shopkeeper is selling goods without a proper trade license, which is a criminal offense. Can a neighbor file a civil suit for specific relief just to make sure the penal law is enforced against the shopkeeper?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Specific relief cannot be granted for the mere purpose of enforcing a penal law.


Part II
OF SPECIFIC RELIEF
Chapter I
OF RECOVERING POSSESSION OF PROPERTY

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can specific relief be granted solely to ensure the enforcement of criminal or penal statutes?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The court is prohibited from granting specific relief when the primary objective is simply the execution of a penal law.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the neighbor's only goal is to enforce a licensing regulation (a penal matter) rather than protecting a civil right, the court must refuse relief under Section 7.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 7, specific relief cannot be granted to the neighbor because the law prohibits using this type of civil remedy solely to enforce a penal or criminal law.

Question: Scenario-based: Five people are caught participating in an unlawful assembly to block a public road. They were not armed and didn't hit anyone. What is the maximum time they can be jailed?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever is a member of an unlawful assembly, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the standard punishment for being a member of an unlawful assembly?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Simple membership in an assembly designated as unlawful is punishable by up to six months of imprisonment or a fine.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the five people were members of an unlawful assembly, the court's sentencing power is limited to the six-month term specified in Section 143.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 143, the maximum punishment for being a member of the unlawful assembly is six months in prison, a fine, or both.

Question: Scenario-based: An eight-year-old girl has been cheated out of her family inheritance. Can the child file a lawsuit herself in her own name, or does an adult have to do it for her?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXII, Rule 1
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every suit by a minor shall be instituted in his name by a person who in such suit shall be called the next friend of the minor.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXII, Rule 1

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: May a minor child initiate a legal action independently or must they be represented by an adult in court?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: All lawsuits on behalf of a minor must be filed by an adult representative known as a 'next friend.'
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The child lacks the legal capacity to sue alone; Rule 1 requires an adult to act as her 'next friend' to officially start the case.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXII, Rule 1, the lawsuit must be started by an adult acting as the child's 'next friend' rather than by the child herself.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order XXXII, Rule 1
