Question: Scenario-based: Arpan tricks his neighbor into signing a document that transfers a car's ownership to him. Is his action considered 'dishonest' under the law if he only intended to gain the car illegally?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever does anything with the intention of causing wrongful gain to one person or wrongful loss to another person, is said to do that thing "dishonestly".

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What criteria must be met for an action to be legally classified as being done 'dishonestly'?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Acting dishonestly requires the specific intent to cause either an illegal gain for someone or an illegal loss to another.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Arpan's intent to acquire the car through trickery constitutes a desire for wrongful gain, satisfying the legal definition of dishonesty.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 24, Arpan acted dishonestly because he performed an act with the intent of causing a wrongful gain of property to himself.

Question: Can I bring any information I want into my trial, or is there a specific rule about what kind of facts the judge will listen to?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Evidence may be given in any suit or proceeding of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other fact as are hereinafter declared to be relevant, and of no others.


Explanation.-This section shall not enable any person to give evidence of a fact which he is disentitled to prove by any provision of the law for the time being in force relating to Civil Procedure.


Illustrations:


(a) A is tried for the murder of B by beating him with a club with the intention of causing his death.


At A's trial the following facts are in issue:–


A's beating B with the club;


A's causing B's death by such beating;


A's intention to cause B's death.


(b) A suitor does not bring with him, and have in readiness for production at the first hearing of the case, a bond on which he relies. This section does not enable him to produce the bond or prove its contents at a subsequent stage of the proceedings, otherwise than in accordance with the conditions prescribed by the Code of Civil Procedure.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What categories of information are legally admissible in a court proceeding?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Evidence is strictly limited to 'facts in issue' and those facts specifically defined as 'relevant' by the Evidence Act.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Section 5 prohibits the introduction of any information that does not qualify as either a fact in issue or a relevant fact under the statutory framework.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 5, you are only allowed to give evidence of 'facts in issue' and facts that the law specifically defines as 'relevant.'

Question: Scenario-based: Before fixing a contract, what must a court be sure about regarding the people who signed it in the first place?

Possible Sections.Full Text: For the purpose of rectifying a contract in writing, the Court must be satisfied that all the parties thereto intended to make an equitable and conscientious agreement.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What standard of intention must be met before a court will correct a written contract?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A court will only fix a contract if it believes everyone involved actually meant to make a fair and honest deal.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Section 32 requires the court to check the character of the original intent—it must be fair (equitable)—before it will agree to change any wording.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 32, the court must be satisfied that everyone involved intended to make a fair and honest (equitable and conscientious) agreement.

Question: Scenario-based: A police officer is trying to stop a large, violent fight in a market. Can the officer demand that a regular shopper nearby help him stop the fight?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 42

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 42

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is there a mandatory legal obligation for private citizens to help stop a breach of the peace when asked by police?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Citizens are legally required to assist law enforcement officers in stopping a violent disturbance or preventing damage to public property when aid is requested.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The market fight is a breach of the peace; therefore, Section 42(b) imposes a binding duty on the shopper to assist the officer if help is reasonably demanded to stop the violence.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 42, Clause (b), every person is legally bound to help a police officer stop a fight or breach of the peace if help is requested.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 42
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: In my trial, I want to testify about the specific angry look on a person's face. Is a person's visible expression considered a 'fact' in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:-


"Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence.


"Fact" means and includes:-


(1) anything, state of things, or relation of things capable of being perceived by the senses;


(2) any mental condition of which any person is conscious.


Illustrations:


(a) That there are certain objects arranged in a certain order in a certain place, is a fact.


(b) That a man heard or saw something, is a fact.


(c) That a man said certain words, is a fact.


(d) That a man holds a certain opinion, has a certain intention, acts in good faith or fraudulently, or uses a particular word in a particular sense, or is or was at a specified time conscious of a particular sensation, is a fact.


(e) That a man has a certain reputation, is a fact.


One fact is said to be relevant to another when the one is connected with the other in any of the ways referred to in the provisions of this Act relating to the relevancy of facts.


The expression "facts in issue" means and includes any fact from which, either by itself or in connection with other facts, the existence, non-existence, nature or extent of any right, liability, or disability, asserted or denied in any suit or proceeding, necessarily follows.


Explanation: Whenever, under the provisions of the law for the time being in force relating to Civil Procedure, any Court records an issue of fact, the fact to be asserted or denied in the answer to such issue is a fact in issue.


Illustrations:


A is accused of the murder of B.


At his trial the following facts may be in issue:-


that A caused B's death;


that A intended to cause B's death;


that A had received grave and sudden provocation from B;


that A, at the time of doing the act which caused B's death, was, by reason of unsoundness of mind, incapable of knowing its nature.


"Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter and includes any digital record.


Illustrations:


A writing is a document.


Words printed, lithographed or photographed are documents.


A map or plan is a document.


An inscription on a metal plate or stone is a document.


A caricature is a document.


"Evidence" means and includes:-


(1) all statements which the Court permits or requires to be made before it by witnesses, in relation to matters of fact under inquiry: such statements are called oral evidence;


(2) all documents produced for the inspection of the Court; such documents are called documentary evidence.


A fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist, or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it exists.


A fact is said to be disproved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes that it does not exist, or considers its non-existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it does not exist.


A fact is said not to be proved when it is neither proved nor disproved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a visible facial expression qualify as a 'fact' under the sensory perception rule?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A fact includes anything that can be perceived by the human senses, including the physical state or appearance of a person.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: An angry look is a physical state capable of being seen; therefore, it qualifies as a fact under the sensory criteria of Section 3.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, Subsection (1), a visible expression is a 'fact.' The law includes anything that can be perceived by the senses within this definition.

Question: Scenario-based: A female suspect needs to be searched for evidence. Can a male police officer perform the search if no female officer is currently at the station?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 52
Possible Sections.Full Text: Whenever it is necessary to cause a woman to be searched, the search shall be made by another woman, with strict regard to decency.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 52

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: May a male officer legally conduct a search of a female suspect when a female officer is unavailable?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The law mandates that any search of a woman must be conducted exclusively by another woman and must maintain strict decency.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Section 52 provides no exception for staff shortages; a male officer is strictly prohibited from searching a female suspect under any circumstances.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 52, a male officer cannot search her; the law strictly requires that a woman must be searched only by another woman.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 52
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: Someone involved in a crime agrees to tell the truth in exchange for a pardon. If they are not already out on bail, can they go home before the trial is completely finished?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 337(3)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) In the case of any offence triable exclusively by the Court of Session, or any offence punishable with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, or any offence punishable under section 211 of the Penal Code, with imprisonment which may extend to seven years, or any offence under any of the following sections of the Penal Code, namely, sections 216A, 369, 401, 435 and 477A, a Metropolitan Magistrate or any Magistrate of the first class may, at any stage of the investigation or inquiry into, or the trial of the offence, with a view to obtaining the evidence of any person supposed to have directly or indirectly concerned in or privy to the offence, tender a pardon to such person on condition of his making a full and true disclosure of the whole of the circumstances within his knowledge relative to the offence and to every other person concerned, whether as principal or abettor, in the commission thereof:


    Provided that, where the offence is under inquiry or trial, no Magistrate of the first class other than the Chief Judicial Magistrate shall exercise the power hereby conferred unless he is the Magistrate making the inquiry or holding the trial, and, where the offence is under investigation, no such Magistrate shall exercise the said power unless he is a Magistrate having jurisdiction in a place where the offence might be inquired into or tried and the sanction of the Chief Judicial Magistrate has been obtained to the exercise thereof.


    (1A) Every Magistrate who tenders a pardon under sub-section (1) shall record his reasons for so doing, and shall, on application made by the accused, furnish him with a copy of such record:


    Provided that the accused shall pay for the same unless the Magistrate for some special reason thinks fit to furnish it free of cost.


    (2) Every person accepting a tender under this section shall be examined as a witness in the Court of the Magistrate taking cognizance of the offence and in the subsequent trial, if any.


    (2A) In every case where a person has accepted a tender of pardon and has been examined under sub-section (2), the Magistrate before whom the proceedings are pending shall, if he is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds for believing that the accused is guilty of an offence, send him for trial to the Court of Session.


    (3) Such person, unless he is already on bail, shall be detained in custody until the termination of the trial.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 337(3)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Must an accomplice who accepts a pardon remain in custody throughout the trial?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A person accepting a tender of pardon must be kept in detention until the trial ends, unless they were already released on bail.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the person is not currently on bail, Section 337, Subsection (3) requires their continued detention until the final conclusion of the entire trial process.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 337, Subsection (3), the person must stay in custody until the trial is completely over, unless they were already out on bail.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 337(3)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A criminal accomplice agrees to tell the truth to get a pardon. If they are not out on bail, are they allowed to go home while the trial is still happening?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 337(3)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) In the case of any offence triable exclusively by the Court of Session, or any offence punishable with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, or any offence punishable under section 211 of the Penal Code, with imprisonment which may extend to seven years, or any offence under any of the following sections of the Penal Code, namely, sections 216A, 369, 401, 435 and 477A, a Metropolitan Magistrate or any Magistrate of the first class may, at any stage of the investigation or inquiry into, or the trial of the offence, with a view to obtaining the evidence of any person supposed to have directly or indirectly concerned in or privy to the offence, tender a pardon to such person on condition of his making a full and true disclosure of the whole of the circumstances within his knowledge relative to the offence and to every other person concerned, whether as principal or abettor, in the commission thereof:


    Provided that, where the offence is under inquiry or trial, no Magistrate of the first class other than the Chief Judicial Magistrate shall exercise the power hereby conferred unless he is the Magistrate making the inquiry or holding the trial, and, where the offence is under investigation, no such Magistrate shall exercise the said power unless he is a Magistrate having jurisdiction in a place where the offence might be inquired into or tried and the sanction of the Chief Judicial Magistrate has been obtained to the exercise thereof.


    (1A) Every Magistrate who tenders a pardon under sub-section (1) shall record his reasons for so doing, and shall, on application made by the accused, furnish him with a copy of such record:


    Provided that the accused shall pay for the same unless the Magistrate for some special reason thinks fit to furnish it free of cost.


    (2) Every person accepting a tender under this section shall be examined as a witness in the Court of the Magistrate taking cognizance of the offence and in the subsequent trial, if any.


    (2A) In every case where a person has accepted a tender of pardon and has been examined under sub-section (2), the Magistrate before whom the proceedings are pending shall, if he is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds for believing that the accused is guilty of an offence, send him for trial to the Court of Session.


    (3) Such person, unless he is already on bail, shall be detained in custody until the termination of the trial.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 337(3)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Must an accomplice accepting a pardon be kept in jail until the trial concludes?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A person who accepts a tender of pardon must remain in detention until the trial ends, unless they were already released on bail.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the accomplice is not on bail, Section 337(3) mandates that they stay in custody for the entire duration of the trial, regardless of their cooperation with the court.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 337, Subsection (3), the person must remain in custody until the trial is completely finished, unless they were already out on bail.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 337(3)

Question: Scenario-based: When a judge is writing a formal charge for 'Murder,' does the judge have to explain every detail, or is just using the word 'Murder' enough?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221(4)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) Every charge under this Code shall state the offence with which the accused is charged.


    (2) If the law which creates the offence gives it any specific name, the offence may be described in the charge by that name only.


    (3) If the law which creates the offence does not give it any specific name, so much of the definition of the offence must be stated as to give the accused notice of the matter with which he is charged.


    (4) The law and section of the law against which the offence is said to have been committed shall be mentioned in the charge.


    (5) The fact that the charge is made is equivalent to a statement that every legal condition required by law to constitute the offence charged was fulfilled in the particular case.


    (6) The charge shall be written either in English or in the language of the Court.


    (7) If the accused having been previously convicted of any offence is liable, by reason of such previous conviction, to enhanced punishment, or to punishment of a different kind, for a subsequent offence, and it is intended to prove such previous conviction for the purpose of affecting the punishment which the Court may think fit to award for the subsequent offence, the fact, date and place of the previous conviction shall be stated in the charge. If such statement has been omitted, the Court may add it at any time before sentence is passed.


    Illustrations:


    (a) A is charged with the murder of B. This equivalent to a statement that A's act fell within the definition of murder given in sections 299 and 300 of the Penal Code; that it did not fall within any of the general exceptions of the same Code; and that it did not fall within any of the five exceptions to section 300, or that, if it did fall within Exception 1, one or other of the three provisions to that exception apply to it.


    (b) A is charged, under section 326 of the Penal Code with voluntarily causing grievous hurt to B by means of an instrument for shooting. This is equivalent to a statement that the case was not provided for by section 335 of the Penal Code, and that the general exceptions did not apply to it.


    (c) A is accused of murder, cheating, theft, extortion, adultery or criminal intimidation, or using a false property-mark. The charge may state that A committed murder, or cheating, or theft, or extortion, or adultery, or criminal intimidation, or that he used a false property-mark, without reference to the definitions of those crimes contained in the Penal Code; but the sections under which the offence is punishable must, in each instance, be referred to in the charge.


    (d) A is charged, under section 184 of the Penal Code with intentionally obstructing a sale of property offered for sale by the lawful authority of a public servant. The charge should be in those words.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221(4)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What are the mandatory elements required to be mentioned in a formal criminal charge document?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A formal charge must explicitly state both the name of the law and the specific section under which the crime falls.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Under Section 221(4), simply writing the facts is not enough; the judge must formally mention 'Section 302 of the Penal Code' to make the murder charge legally valid.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 221, Subsection (4), the judge must mention the specific name of the law and the section number in the formal charge document.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221(4)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A person involved in a gang robbery agrees to tell the truth for a pardon. If the person later lies in court, can they still use that pardon to escape punishment?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 337(2)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) In the case of any offence triable exclusively by the Court of Session, or any offence punishable with imprisonment which may extend to ten years, or any offence punishable under section 211 of the Penal Code, with imprisonment which may extend to seven years, or any offence under any of the following sections of the Penal Code, namely, sections 216A, 369, 401, 435 and 477A, a Metropolitan Magistrate or any Magistrate of the first class may, at any stage of the investigation or inquiry into, or the trial of the offence, with a view to obtaining the evidence of any person supposed to have directly or indirectly concerned in or privy to the offence, tender a pardon to such person on condition of his making a full and true disclosure of the whole of the circumstances within his knowledge relative to the offence and to every other person concerned, whether as principal or abettor, in the commission thereof:


    Provided that, where the offence is under inquiry or trial, no Magistrate of the first class other than the Chief Judicial Magistrate shall exercise the power hereby conferred unless he is the Magistrate making the inquiry or holding the trial, and, where the offence is under investigation, no such Magistrate shall exercise the said power unless he is a Magistrate having jurisdiction in a place where the offence might be inquired into or tried and the sanction of the Chief Judicial Magistrate has been obtained to the exercise thereof.


    (1A) Every Magistrate who tenders a pardon under sub-section (1) shall record his reasons for so doing, and shall, on application made by the accused, furnish him with a copy of such record:


    Provided that the accused shall pay for the same unless the Magistrate for some special reason thinks fit to furnish it free of cost.


    (2) Every person accepting a tender under this section shall be examined as a witness in the Court of the Magistrate taking cognizance of the offence and in the subsequent trial, if any.


    (2A) In every case where a person has accepted a tender of pardon and has been examined under sub-section (2), the Magistrate before whom the proceedings are pending shall, if he is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds for believing that the accused is guilty of an offence, send him for trial to the Court of Session.


    (3) Such person, unless he is already on bail, shall be detained in custody until the termination of the trial.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 337(2)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the mandatory duty of a person who has accepted a legal pardon in exchange for testimony?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Any person who accepts a pardon must fulfill the condition of providing full and true evidence as a witness during the trial.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Under Section 337(2), the person's right to the pardon depends on their testimony; if they lie, they fail the condition of the pardon and can be prosecuted for the original crime.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 337, Subsection (2), the person must testify truthfully in court as a witness to keep their pardon; failing to do so cancels the deal.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 337(2)
