(o) A is the paramour of Z's wife. She gives A valuable property, which A knows to belong to her husband Z, and to be such property as she has not authority from Z to give. If A takes the property dishonestly, he commits theft.


(p) A, in good faith, believing property belonging to Z to be A's own property, takes that property out of B's possession. Here, as A does not take dishonestly, he does not commit theft.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the act of moving property to steal it complete the offense even if the property is not removed?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Theft is completed as soon as property is moved with a dishonest intent to take it without the owner's consent.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: By cutting the tree, Javed severed and moved it; this physical act fulfills the final requirement for the commission of theft.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 378, Explanation 2, Javed has committed theft because the law states that the act of severing property from the earth constitutes moving it, which completes the crime.

Question: Scenario-based: A police officer sees a group of men about to vandalize a public building. Is the officer legally required to step in before the crime actually happens?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 149
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every police-officer may interpose for the purpose of preventing, and shall, to the best of his ability, prevent, the commission of any cognizable offence.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 149

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a police officer have a mandatory duty to intervene to prevent the commission of a cognizable offence?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: All police officers are legally obligated to use their best efforts to interpose and prevent any cognizable crime from taking place.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Vandalism is a cognizable offence; therefore, Section 149 imposes a mandatory legal duty on the officer to prevent the men from completing the act.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 149, every police officer has a mandatory duty to prevent the commission of any serious crime to the best of their ability.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 149
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A shopkeeper sold and delivered some electronics to a customer today, but they didn't agree on a specific date for payment. From what day does the three-year limit to sue for the money start?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 52
Possible Sections.Full Text: For the price of goods sold and delivered, where no fixed period of credit is agreed upon. Three years.. The date of the delivery of the goods.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 52

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When does the limitation period begin for a suit to recover the price of goods sold without a credit agreement?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The three-year period to sue for payment begins on the exact date the goods are delivered to the buyer.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because no credit period was fixed, Article 52 triggers the limitation clock immediately. The shopkeeper's three-year window to file a lawsuit starts today, the date of delivery.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 52, the three-year deadline to sue for the money starts today — the law counts from the exact date the goods were delivered to the customer.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 52

Question: Scenario-based: An accused person is very old and unable to travel to the court. Can the judge allow the person's lawyer to appear for them instead?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 205(1)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) Whenever a Magistrate issues a summons, he may, if he sees reason so to do, dispense with the personal attendance of the accused, and permit him to appear by his pleader.


    (2) But the Magistrate inquiring into or trying the case may, in his discretion, at any stage of the proceedings, direct the personal attendance of the accused, and, if necessary, enforce such attendance in manner hereinbefore provided.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 205(1)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a magistrate permitted to waive the requirement for an accused person to attend court in person?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The court has the discretion to exempt an accused person from appearing personally and allow their lawyer to represent them.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Under Section 205(1), the judge can use their discretion for the elderly accused person and allow the lawyer to appear in their place to handle the hearing.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 205, Subsection (1), the judge has the power to allow an accused person to send a lawyer instead of appearing in person.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 205(1)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A prisoner is sentenced to death. The government wants to change this sentence to life imprisonment. Does the government need the prisoner's consent to make this change?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In every case in which sentence of death shall have been passed, the Government may, without the consent of the offender, commute the punishment for any other punishment provided by this Code.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is the state's power to reduce a death sentence dependent on the convict's agreement?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The executive authority possesses the unilateral power to substitute a death penalty with another legal punishment without seeking the offender's approval.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The government may exercise its discretion to commute the sentence, and the prisoner's lack of consent does not block this decision.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 54, the government does not need the prisoner's permission; it has the sole power to change a death sentence to any other punishment provided by the Code.

Question: Scenario-based: A tells B to steal a watch. B tells C to steal that same watch. C finally steals it. Is A guilty of abetment even though he never spoke to C?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
A person abets an offence, who abets either the commission of an offence, or the commission of an act which would be an offence, if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence with the same intention or knowledge as that of the abettor.


Explanation 1.-The abetment of the illegal omission of an act may amount to an offence although the abettor may not himself be bound to do that act.


Explanation 2.-To constitute the offence of abetment it is not necessary that the act abetted should be committed, or that the effect requisite to constitute the offence should be caused.


Illustrations:
(a) A instigates B to murder C. B refuses to do so. A is guilty of abetting B to commit murder.
(b) A instigates B to murder D. B in pursuance of the instigation stabs D. D recovers from the wound. A is guilty of instigating B to commit murder.


Explanation 3.-It is not necessary that the person abetted should be capable by law of committing an offence, or that he should have the same guilty intention or knowledge as that of the abettor, or any guilty intention or knowledge.


Illustrations:
(a) A, with a guilty intention, abets a child or a lunatic to commit an act which would be an offence, if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence, and having the same intention as A. Here A, whether the act be committed or not, is guilty of abetting an offence.
(b) A, with the intention of murdering Z, instigates B, a child under seven years of age, to do an act which causes Z's death. B, in consequence of the abetment, does the act in the absence of A and thereby, causes Z's death. Here, though B was not capable by law of committing an offence, A is liable to be punished in the same manner as if B had been capable by law of committing an offence, and had committed murder, and he is therefore subject to the punishment of death.
(c) A instigates B to set fire to a dwelling-house. B, in consequence of the unsoundness of his mind, being incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is wrong or contrary to law, sets fire to the house in consequence of A's instigation. B has committed no offence, but A is guilty of abetting the offence of setting fire to a dwelling-house, and is liable to the punishment provided for that offence.
(d) A intending to cause a theft to be committed, instigates B to take property belonging to Z out of Z's possession. A induces B to believe that the property belongs to A. B takes the property out of Z's possession, in good faith, believing it to be A's property. B, acting under this misconception, does not take dishonestly, and therefore does not commit theft. But A is guilty of abetting theft, and is liable to the same punishment as if B had committed theft.


Explanation 4.-The abetment of an offence being an offence, the abetment of such an abetment is also an offence.


Illustration:
A instigates B to instigate C to murder Z. B accordingly instigates C to murder Z, and C commits that offence in consequence of B's instigation. B is liable to be punished for his offence with the punishment for murder; and, as A instigated B to commit the offence, A is also liable to the same punishment.


Explanation 5.-It is not necessary to the commission of the offence of abetment by conspiracy that the abettor should concert the offence with the person who commits it. It is sufficient if he engages in the conspiracy in pursuance of which the offence is committed.


Illustration:
A concerts with B a plan for poisoning Z. It is agreed that A shall administer the poison. B then explains the plan to C mentioning that a third person is to administer the poison, but without mentioning A's name. C agrees to procure the poison, and procures and delivers it to B for the purpose of its being used in the manner explained. A administers the poison; Z dies in consequence. Here, though A and C have not conspired together, yet C has been engaged in the conspiracy in pursuance of which Z has been murdered. C has therefore committed the offence defined in this section and is liable to the punishment for murder.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can an individual be liable for abetment if they are the original instigator in a chain of abetment?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The law defines the abetment of an abetment as a punishable offense in itself.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: A instigated B to abet C; therefore, A is liable for the theft under the rule that the first link in the abetment chain is legally responsible.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 108, Explanation 4, A is guilty of abetment because the law states that the abetment of an abetment is also a crime.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim wants to sue the government because of a land compensation error. When he fills out the court forms, what official name should he use for the defendant?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 79(a)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
In a suit by or against the Government the authority to be named as plaintiff or defendant, as the case may be, shall be Bangladesh-


[* * *]

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 79(a)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the correct legal name to use when initiating a lawsuit against the national government?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: In any legal action involving the government, the party must be officially named as 'Bangladesh' on all court documents.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Rahim must follow the naming convention in Section 79(a) and list the defendant simply as 'Bangladesh' on all his official court filings for the case.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 79, Clause (a), when suing the government, you must name the defendant as 'Bangladesh' in your court papers.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 79(a)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A woodcutter is working with an axe when the head suddenly flies off and kills someone standing nearby. He was using proper care. Is he guilty of a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.


Illustration:
A is at work with a hatchet; the head flies off and kills a man who is standing by. Here if there was no want of proper caution on the part of A, his act is excusable and not an offence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person criminally liable for a death caused by an unfortunate accident while performing a lawful task with due care?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Actions resulting from accidents are not offenses if performed lawfully, without criminal intent, and with appropriate caution.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The woodcutter's act was an accident during lawful work performed with due care, satisfying all conditions for a general exception to liability.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 80, the woodcutter is not guilty because he was performing a lawful act with proper care and the death was caused by an accident.

Question: Scenario-based: A tourist from France steals an expensive watch from a shopping mall in Dhaka. Can he be punished under the laws of Bangladesh, or is he exempt because he is a foreign citizen?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Every person shall be liable to punishment under this Code and not otherwise for every act or omission contrary to the provisions thereof, of which he shall be guilty within 
Bangladesh.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the Code apply to every individual who commits an offense within the borders of Bangladesh?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Any person, regardless of nationality, remains subject to legal penalties for prohibited acts or omissions performed inside national territory.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the theft occurred in Dhaka, the tourist is physically present within the country's limits, thus activating the territorial jurisdiction.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 2, the tourist is liable because the law applies to every person committing an offense within Bangladesh, regardless of their nationality or background.

Question: Scenario-based: A man creates fake coins that look very similar to real ones. He claims he isn't 'counterfeiting' because the copies aren't perfect. Does the law require an exact copy?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
A person is said to "counterfeit" who causes one thing to resemble another thing, intending by means of that resemblance to practice deception, or knowing it to be likely that deception will thereby be practiced.


Explanation 1.- It is not essential to counterfeiting that the imitation should be exact.


Explanation 2.- When a person causes one thing to resemble another thing, and resemblance is such that a person might be deceived thereby, it shall be presumed, until the contrary is proved, that the person so causing the one thing to resemble the other thing intended by means of that resemblance to practice deception or knew it to be likely that deception would thereby be practiced

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an exact imitation required for an act to be classified as counterfeiting?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Counterfeiting occurs when someone creates a resemblance with intent to deceive, and the law does not require the copy to be perfect.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The man's similar coins satisfy the 'resemblance' requirement; the first explanation explicitly rejects his defense that the imitation must be exact.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 28, Explanation 1, the man's claim is wrong because the law states that an imitation does not need to be exact to count as counterfeiting.

Question: Scenario-based: A woman lost a case regarding a business debt. The person who won wants the court to arrest her and put her in jail until she pays. Can the judge order her arrest for this money debt?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 56
Possible Sections.Full Text: Notwithstanding anything in this Part, the Court shall not order the arrest or detention in the civil prison of 37[an old, infirm, pregnant or breast-feeding woman] in execution of a decree for the payment of money.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 56

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is the court prohibited from arresting a female debtor to enforce a judgment involving the payment of money?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The law explicitly forbids the civil arrest or imprisonment of women as a means to execute money-related court orders.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the decree involves a business debt (payment of money), Section 56 bars the court from ordering her arrest or detention.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 56, the judge is strictly prohibited from ordering the arrest or imprisonment of a woman to enforce a decree for the payment of money.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 56
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A landlord is suing an agent for not collecting rent from a tenant. If the tenant admitted to someone else that they did indeed owe the rent, can that statement be used as evidence against the agent?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
When there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact.


Illustrations:


(a) The question is, whether a particular letter was dispatched.


The facts that it was the ordinary course of business for all letters put in a certain place to be carried to the post, and that that particular letter was put in that place are relevant.


(b) The question is, whether particular letter reached A.


The facts that it was posted in due course, and was not returned through the Dead Letter office, are relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a third party's admission of a debt relevant in a lawsuit against an agent responsible for collecting that debt?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A statement made by a third person regarding a debt is an admission and a relevant fact against an agent charged with collecting that debt.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The tenant’s admission that rent was owed is a fact that triggers Section 16, Illustration (b), making it a relevant admission against the agent in court.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 16, Illustration (b), the tenant's statement about owing rent is an admission that can be used as a relevant fact against the agent.

Question: Scenario-based: The Government of Bangladesh wants to sue a person to recover some public land that has been occupied illegally. Does the Government have the same 12-year deadline as a regular citizen?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 149
Possible Sections.Full Text: Every police-officer may interpose for the purpose of preventing, and shall, to the best of his ability, prevent, the commission of any cognizable offence.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 149

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for lawsuits initiated by the Government?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Almost all civil suits brought by or on behalf of the Government are subject to a much longer limitation period of sixty years.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Unlike private citizens who face a 12-year limit for land recovery, Article 149 grants the Government sixty years to file its lawsuit for the recovery of the occupied public land.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 149, the Government has sixty years to file its lawsuit — this is much longer than the deadline allowed for private citizens.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 149

Question: Scenario-based: A woman helps a soldier desert from the Bangladesh Navy by providing him with a hideout and a fake ID. What is the longest time she can be jailed for helping him leave his service?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever abets the desertion of any officer, soldier, sailor or airman, in the Army, Navy or Air Force of Bangladesh, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the statutory penalty for abetting a military member's desertion?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Encouraging or assisting a member of the armed forces to desert their post is a crime punishable by up to two years in prison.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: By providing a hideout and ID to facilitate the sailor's desertion, the woman has abetted the act, triggering the two-year maximum under Section 135.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 135, the woman can be sentenced to a maximum of two years in prison, or a fine, or both.

Question: Scenario-based: Someone bought a house at a court auction. The sale was officially finalized and made absolute today. How long do they have to ask the court to help them get possession from the previous owner?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 180
Possible Sections.Full Text: By a purchaser of immoveable property at a sale in execution of a decree for delivery of possession. Three days When the sale becomes absolute.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 180

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a purchaser at a court execution sale to apply for delivery of possession?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The purchaser has a very short window of three days to apply for possession once the sale is made absolute.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the sale became absolute today, Article 180 triggers a strict three-day deadline. The purchaser must submit their application for delivery of possession by the end of the third day from now.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 180, the purchaser has only three days to apply for possession — this time limit starts exactly when the sale becomes absolute.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 180

Question: Scenario-based: Three people meet and agree to carry out a major bank robbery. Can a police officer arrest them for this conspiracy without a warrant from a judge?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 120B
Possible Sections.Full Text: Criminal conspiracy to commit an offence punishable with death, transportation or rigorous imprisonment for a term of two years or upwards.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 120B

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a charge of criminal conspiracy allow for a warrantless arrest if the target crime is serious?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Warrantless arrest for conspiracy is permitted only if the crime being plotted also allows for arrest without a warrant.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since bank robbery is a serious cognizable crime allowing warrantless arrest, Section 120B of Schedule 2 authorizes the police to arrest the conspirators immediately without a warrant.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 120B, the police can arrest for conspiracy without a warrant if the robbery they are planning is also a crime allowing warrantless arrest.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 120B
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A man is serving a life sentence. The Government decides to commute his sentence to a fixed term of years. What is the maximum number of years the Government can set for this new sentence?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In every case in which sentence of imprisonment for life shall have been passed, the Government may, without the consent of the offender, commute the punishment for imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding twenty years.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the maximum statutory limit for a commuted life sentence?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The executive power to commute life imprisonment is restricted to a maximum term of twenty years of rigorous or simple imprisonment.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: While the Government can reduce the sentence, it must not exceed the twenty-year cap established by Section 55 of the Code.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 55, the Government can commute the life sentence to a fixed term, but that new term cannot exceed twenty years.

Question: Scenario-based: A woman discovered today that the contract she signed last year was based on a lie. She wants to cancel the contract. How much time does she have from today to start a lawsuit for this?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 114
Possible Sections.Full Text: For the rescission of a contract. 1 [One year] When the facts entitling the plaintiff to have the contract rescinded first become known to him.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 114

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for the rescission of a contract from the date the grounds for rescission are discovered?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A suit to cancel or rescind a contract must be brought within one year of the plaintiff learning the facts that allow for the cancellation.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the woman discovered the lie (the grounds for rescission) today, Article 114 sets a one-year deadline starting from this moment for her to file her lawsuit in court.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 114, the woman has exactly one year to file her lawsuit — this time limit starts from today, when she first learned the facts about the lie.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 114

Question: Scenario-based: A man sets fire to a house at night to help with a robbery. He didn't intend to kill anyone, but a person dies in the fire. Did he cause the death 'voluntarily'?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
A person is said to cause an effect "voluntarily" when he causes it by means whereby he intended to cause it, or by means which, at the time of employing those means, he knew or had reason to believe to be likely to cause it.


Illustration:


A sets fire, by night, to an inhabited house in a large town, for the purpose of facilitating robbery and thus causes the death of a person. Here, A may not have intended to cause death, and may even be sorry that death has been caused by his act: yet, if he knew that he was likely to cause death, he has caused death voluntarily.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a death 'voluntary' if the actor knew it was likely, even if they did not specifically intend it?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: An effect is caused voluntarily if the person uses means they know or believe are likely to produce that result.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: By setting fire to an inhabited house, the man used means likely to cause death, fulfilling the legal definition of acting voluntarily.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 39, the death was caused voluntarily because the man used means that he knew were likely to result in death, even if he didn't specifically intend it.

Question: Scenario-based: A family was living on a plot of land for years but was forced off by a neighbor in 2020. They want to get it back now. How many years from that event do they have to sue?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 142
Possible Sections.Full Text: For possession of immoveable property when the plaintiff, while in possession of the property, has been dispossessed or has discontinued the possession. Twelve years.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 142

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for recovering possession of immoveable property after being forced out?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A suit for possession of land following dispossession must be brought within twelve years of the date the plaintiff was removed.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The family was dispossessed in 2020. Under Article 142, they have a twelve-year window, meaning they must file their lawsuit by 2032 to legally recover their property.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 142, the family has twelve years to sue for possession — the time limit began in 2020 when they were forced off the land.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 142

Question: Scenario-based: A police officer is ordered by a judge to arrest 'Z'. After a careful check, the officer arrests 'A', truly believing 'A' is actually 'Z'. Has the officer committed a crime by arresting the wrong person?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith believes himself to be, bound by law to do it.


Illustrations:
(a) A, a soldier, fires on a mob by the order of his superior officer, in conformity with the commands of the law. A has committed no offence.
(b) A, an officer of a Court of Justice, being ordered by that Court to arrest Y, and, after due enquiry, believing Z to be Y, arrests Z. A has committed no offence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person legally protected if they commit an act based on a factual error while believing they are following the law?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Actions are not offenses if the individual acted in good faith due to a mistake of fact and was legally bound to the duty.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The officer's honest belief that 'A' was 'Z' constitutes a valid mistake of fact, granting him immunity from criminal liability.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 76, the officer has not committed a crime because he acted in good faith and made a mistake of fact, believing he was legally bound to arrest the person.

Question: Scenario-based: During a lawsuit over a business, the court needs someone neutral to manage the company's property. Which set of rules will decide exactly how that person is appointed?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
The appointment of a receiver pending a suit is a matter resting in the discretion of the Court.


    The mode and effect of his appointment, and his rights, powers, duties and liabilities, are regulated by the Code of Civil Procedure.
Chapter VIII

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which legal code governs the rules and duties for a court-appointed receiver?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The appointment, powers, and duties of a receiver are managed according to the Code of Civil Procedure.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Section 44 points directly to the Code of Civil Procedure as the regulating law for anything involving court-appointed receivers.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 44, the Code of Civil Procedure is the law that explains how a receiver is appointed and what their powers are.

Question: Scenario-based: A man was excluded from using his family's joint property five years ago. He only found out about this exclusion today. How much time does he have to sue to get his share?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 127
Possible Sections.Full Text: By a person excluded from joint family property to enforce a right to share therein. Twelve years.. When the exclusion becomes known to the plaintiff.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 127

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When does the limitation period begin for a suit to enforce a share in joint family property after being excluded?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A person has twelve years to sue for their share of joint family property, starting from the day they first learn about their exclusion.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Even though the exclusion happened five years ago, the man only learned of it today. Under Article 127, his twelve-year limitation period begins now, on the date of his discovery.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 127, the man has twelve years to sue for his share — the clock only starts today, since this is when he first learned he was excluded.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 127

Question: Scenario-based: Bina is suspected of carrying stolen jewelry in her dress. A male police officer wants to search her immediately at the scene. Is he legally allowed to do this search himself?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 52
Possible Sections.Full Text: Whenever it is necessary to cause a woman to be searched, the search shall be made by another woman, with strict regard to decency.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 52

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a male officer legally perform a physical search on a female suspect for evidence recovery?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Searches of female individuals must be conducted exclusively by another woman and must maintain a high standard of respectability.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because Bina is a woman, Section 52 mandates that the male officer refrain from searching her. Only a female officer or person can legally perform the search while ensuring her decency is protected.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 52, the male officer cannot search her. The law strictly requires that any search of a woman must be done by another woman with strict regard to her decency.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 52
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim sells his land but then cuts down and takes the trees that were growing on it. He claims the trees are part of the land and are not 'moveable property.' Is his claim correct?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The words "moveable property" are intended to include corporeal property of every description, except land and things attached to the earth or permanently fastened to anything which is attached to the earth.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are things attached to the earth excluded from the definition of moveable property?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Property is considered moveable if it consists of physical items, excluding land and anything permanently fixed to the ground.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As trees were attached to the earth, they fall under the exclusion until severed; the definition confirms land isn't moveable.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 22, Rahim is partially correct because the law excludes land and things attached to the earth from being classified as moveable property until they are actually severed.

Question: Scenario-based: A citizen sees someone climbing through a window at night and, thinking it is a burglar, tackles him to prevent a crime. It turns out the person was actually the homeowner who lost his keys. Is the citizen guilty of assault?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Nothing is an offence which is done by any person who is justified by law, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith, believes himself to be justified by law, in doing it.


Illustration:
A sees Z commit what appears to A to be a murder. A, in the exercise, to the best of his judgment, exerted in good faith of the power which the law gives to all persons of apprehending murderers in the act, seizes Z, in order to bring Z before the proper authorities. A has committed no offence, though it may turn out that Z was acting in self-defence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a mistake of fact excuse a person who believes their illegal act is justified by law?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: An act is not an offense if the person is either justified by law or believes so in good faith due to a factual error.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The citizen acted on a good-faith mistake of fact, believing his intervention was a legally justified arrest of a burglar.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 79, the citizen is not guilty because he acted in good faith based on a mistake of fact and believed he was justified by law.

Question: Scenario-based: Two people signed a business contract while they were in France. Now they are having a dispute and one is suing the other in a court in Bangladesh. Which country's time limit rules apply to this case?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) Suits instituted in Bangladesh on contracts entered into in a foreign country are subject to the rules of limitation contained in this Act. (2) No foreign rule of limitation shall be a defence to a suit instituted in Bangladesh on a contract entered into in a foreign country, unless the rule has extinguished the contract and the parties were domiciled in such country during the period prescribed by such rule.
Part III
COMPUTATION OF PERIOD OF LIMITATION

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which limitation laws apply to a lawsuit filed in Bangladesh concerning a contract made in a foreign country?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Lawsuits filed within Bangladesh for foreign contracts must follow the limitation periods established by the Bangladesh Limitation Act.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Even though the contract was signed in France, the legal action is taking place in Bangladesh. Under Section 11(1), the court must apply the time limits of the Bangladesh Limitation Act, 1908.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 11, Subsection (1), the case must follow the time limit rules of Bangladesh, even though the contract was originally signed in France.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim is accused of burning a shop during a riot. Can the prosecutor mention that a mob smashed a nearby car at the same time, even if Rahim wasn't near the car?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.


Illustrations:


(a) A is accused of the murder of B by beating him. Whatever was said or done by A or B or the bystanders at the beating, or so shortly before or after it as to form part of the transaction, is a relevant fact.


(b) A is accused of waging war against Bangladesh by taking part in an armed insurrection in which property is destroyed, troops are attacked, and jails are broken open. The occurrence of these facts is relevant, as forming part of the general transaction, though A may not have been present at all of them.


(c) A sues B for a libel contained in a letter forming part of a correspondence. Letters between the parties relating to the subject out of which the libel arose, and forming part of the correspondence in which it is contained, are relevant facts, though they do not contain the libel itself.


(d) The question is whether certain goods ordered from B were delivered to A. The goods were delivered to several intermediate persons successively. Each delivery is a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are separate acts of violence relevant if they form part of a single general transaction or riot?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Facts connected to a main issue as part of the same transaction are relevant even if they happened in different places or the accused was absent.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The car smashing is part of the same riot transaction as the shop burning; therefore, Section 6 makes the car incident relevant to Rahim's case.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 6, Illustration (b), the smashed car can be mentioned. It is part of the same general transaction as the shop burning, making it relevant evidence.

Question: Scenario-based: A court cancels a contract because it was unfair to one side. Can the judge also order the person who got the contract cancelled to pay some money to the other person for their troubles?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
On adjudging the rescission of a contract, the Court may require the party to whom such relief is granted to make any compensation to the other which justice may require.


Chapter V
OF THE CANCELLATION OF INSTRUMENTS

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a court have the power to order the winning party in a rescission suit to compensate the losing party?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: When granting rescission, the court has the discretion to order the benefited party to provide compensation to ensure a just outcome.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Section 38 gives the judge authority to balance the cancellation with a monetary payment if 'justice requires' that the other party be compensated.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 38, the court can order the person who won the case to pay compensation to the other side if the judge believes it is necessary for fairness.

Question: Scenario-based: During a riot, Kamal is carrying a sharp sword, while the others are unarmed. Does Kamal face a longer sentence than his companions?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever is guilty of rioting, being armed with a deadly weapon or with anything which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is there an enhanced penalty for being armed with a deadly weapon during a riot?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Rioters who carry deadly weapons are subject to a maximum sentence of three years, compared to two years for unarmed rioters.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Kamal's possession of a sword during the riot triggers the enhanced three-year penalty prescribed by Section 148.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 148, Kamal faces a harsher sentence of up to three years in prison because he was armed with a deadly weapon during the riot.

Question: Scenario-based: An elderly person is accused of a minor crime. If they are too sick to come to court, can the judge let their lawyer appear and answer questions for them?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 205(1)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) Whenever a Magistrate issues a summons, he may, if he sees reason so to do, dispense with the personal attendance of the accused, and permit him to appear by his pleader.


    (2) But the Magistrate inquiring into or trying the case may, in his discretion, at any stage of the proceedings, direct the personal attendance of the accused, and, if necessary, enforce such attendance in manner hereinbefore provided.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 205(1)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a magistrate permitted to allow an accused person to skip their court appearance and send a lawyer instead?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The court may, at its discretion, exempt the accused from appearing in person and allow them to be represented by their legal pleader.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the accused is ill, Section 205(1) empowers the magistrate to dispense with their physical presence and permit a lawyer to appear on their behalf.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 205, Subsection (1), the judge has the power to allow an accused person to send a lawyer instead of appearing in person.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 205(1)

Question: Scenario-based: A court is formally charging someone with a crime. By doing this, is the court essentially saying that every legal requirement for that crime has been met?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221(5)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) Every charge under this Code shall state the offence with which the accused is charged.


    (2) If the law which creates the offence gives it any specific name, the offence may be described in the charge by that name only.


    (3) If the law which creates the offence does not give it any specific name, so much of the definition of the offence must be stated as to give the accused notice of the matter with which he is charged.


    (4) The law and section of the law against which the offence is said to have been committed shall be mentioned in the charge.


    (5) The fact that the charge is made is equivalent to a statement that every legal condition required by law to constitute the offence charged was fulfilled in the particular case.


    (6) The charge shall be written either in English or in the language of the Court.


    (7) If the accused having been previously convicted of any offence is liable, by reason of such previous conviction, to enhanced punishment, or to punishment of a different kind, for a subsequent offence, and it is intended to prove such previous conviction for the purpose of affecting the punishment which the Court may think fit to award for the subsequent offence, the fact, date and place of the previous conviction shall be stated in the charge. If such statement has been omitted, the Court may add it at any time before sentence is passed.


    Illustrations:


    (a) A is charged with the murder of B. This equivalent to a statement that A's act fell within the definition of murder given in sections 299 and 300 of the Penal Code; that it did not fall within any of the general exceptions of the same Code; and that it did not fall within any of the five exceptions to section 300, or that, if it did fall within Exception 1, one or other of the three provisions to that exception apply to it.


    (b) A is charged, under section 326 of the Penal Code with voluntarily causing grievous hurt to B by means of an instrument for shooting. This is equivalent to a statement that the case was not provided for by section 335 of the Penal Code, and that the general exceptions did not apply to it.


    (c) A is accused of murder, cheating, theft, extortion, adultery or criminal intimidation, or using a false property-mark. The charge may state that A committed murder, or cheating, or theft, or extortion, or adultery, or criminal intimidation, or that he used a false property-mark, without reference to the definitions of those crimes contained in the Penal Code; but the sections under which the offence is punishable must, in each instance, be referred to in the charge.


    (d) A is charged, under section 184 of the Penal Code with intentionally obstructing a sale of property offered for sale by the lawful authority of a public servant. The charge should be in those words.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221(5)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the legal effect of a court officially framing a charge against an accused person?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Framing a charge is equivalent to a formal declaration that every legal condition required for the offense has been met in the case.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: By issuing the charge, the court satisfies Section 221(5), essentially stating that there is enough legal basis to suggest the crime was actually committed by the person.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 221, Subsection (5), the act of framing a charge means the court is stating that all legal requirements for the crime were fulfilled.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 221(5)

Question: Scenario-based: A man accidentally kills someone because he was playing with a loaded gun that he thought was empty. He didn't mean to kill him. Can he be punished for this death?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person liable for a death caused by extreme carelessness rather than intentional homicide?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Causing death through a rash or negligent act that does not meet the criteria for homicide is a punishable offense.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Playing with a loaded gun is a classic rash and negligent act; while unintentional, the resulting death is punishable by up to five years under Section 304A.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 304A, the man can be punished with up to five years in prison for causing a death through his rash and negligent actions.

Question: Scenario-based: I confessed after being threatened, but then the police made sure I felt totally safe and free from that threat before I confessed a second time. Is the second confession allowed?

Possible Sections.Full Text: If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession relevant if the initial illegal threat has been completely neutralized before the statement is made?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A confession becomes relevant if the court is satisfied that the impression from a prior threat or promise was fully removed before the confession.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the police ensured the threat was 'fully removed' before the second confession, it becomes relevant and admissible under Section 28.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 28, the second confession is relevant because it was made after the threat that caused the first confession had been fully removed.

Question: I am suing someone for an insulting letter they wrote to me. Can I show the judge the other friendly letters we sent back and forth earlier to help explain our relationship?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.


Illustrations:


(a) A is accused of the murder of B by beating him. Whatever was said or done by A or B or the bystanders at the beating, or so shortly before or after it as to form part of the transaction, is a relevant fact.


(b) A is accused of waging war against Bangladesh by taking part in an armed insurrection in which property is destroyed, troops are attacked, and jails are broken open. The occurrence of these facts is relevant, as forming part of the general transaction, though A may not have been present at all of them.


(c) A sues B for a libel contained in a letter forming part of a correspondence. Letters between the parties relating to the subject out of which the libel arose, and forming part of the correspondence in which it is contained, are relevant facts, though they do not contain the libel itself.


(d) The question is whether certain goods ordered from B were delivered to A. The goods were delivered to several intermediate persons successively. Each delivery is a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are non-libelous letters from the same correspondence chain relevant in a libel lawsuit?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Documents that form part of the same correspondence or transaction as the fact in issue are relevant, even if they don't contain the primary dispute.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The friendly letters are part of the same correspondence history as the insulting letter, making them relevant 'connected facts' under Section 6, Illustration (c).
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 6, Illustration (c), those earlier letters are relevant because they are part of the same ongoing correspondence as the insulting letter.

Question: Scenario-based: I lost my original house contract. Can I just testify in court about what was written in that document, or will the judge refuse to hear me?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Oral admissions as to the contents of a document are not relevant, unless and until that the party proposing to prove them shows that he is entitled to give secondary evidence of the contents of such document under the rules hereinafter contained, or unless the genuineness of a document produced is in question.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When is oral testimony about the contents of a document admissible as an admission?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Oral statements about what a document contains are irrelevant unless the party first proves they are legally entitled to provide secondary evidence.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the contract is lost, the client must first satisfy the court they are 'entitled to give secondary evidence' before Section 22 allows their oral testimony.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 22, you cannot testify about what was in the contract unless you first prove to the court that you are legally allowed to use secondary evidence.

Question: Scenario-based: A judge in Dhaka needs to get a statement from a witness who lives in a foreign country. Since the judge has no power outside Bangladesh, what official document can he send to request help from the foreign court?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 77
Possible Sections.Full Text: In lieu of issuing a commission the Court may issue a letter of request to examine a witness residing at any place not within Bangladesh.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 77

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the proper legal instrument for requesting a foreign court to assist in examining a witness residing outside national borders?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A court may issue a formal 'letter of request' to a foreign jurisdiction to facilitate the examination of witnesses living abroad.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the witness is in a foreign country, Section 77 authorizes the Dhaka judge to issue a letter of request to obtain the necessary testimony.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 77, the judge can issue a 'letter of request' to ask the foreign court for assistance in examining the witness residing abroad.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 77
