Question: Scenario-based: A house was robbed. Can the police use the fact that the owner left the back door unlocked as evidence to show how the crime happened?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Facts which are the occasions, cause, or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.


Illustrations:


(a) The question is whether A robbed B.


The facts that, shortly before the robbery, B went to a fair with money in his possession and that he showed it or mentioned the fact that he had it to third persons, are relevant.


(b) The question is whether A murdered B.


Marks on the ground, produced by a struggle at or near the place where the murder was committed, are relevant facts.


(c) The question is whether A poisoned B.


The state of B's health before the symptoms ascribed to poison, and habits of B, known to A, which afforded an opportunity for the administration of poison, are relevant facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a fact that provided an opportunity for a crime considered legally relevant?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Any fact that constitutes the state of things or affords an opportunity for an event to happen is relevant evidence.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Leaving the back door unlocked directly 'afforded an opportunity' for the robbery to occur, making that fact relevant under the criteria of Section 7.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 7, the fact that the door was left unlocked is relevant because it afforded an opportunity for the robbery to occur.

Question: If a man has a reputation for being very honest in his village, is that specific reputation considered a 'fact' under the law?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:-


"Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence.


"Fact" means and includes:-


(1) anything, state of things, or relation of things capable of being perceived by the senses;


(2) any mental condition of which any person is conscious.


Illustrations:


(a) That there are certain objects arranged in a certain order in a certain place, is a fact.


(b) That a man heard or saw something, is a fact.


(c) That a man said certain words, is a fact.


(d) That a man holds a certain opinion, has a certain intention, acts in good faith or fraudulently, or uses a particular word in a particular sense, or is or was at a specified time conscious of a particular sensation, is a fact.


(e) That a man has a certain reputation, is a fact.


One fact is said to be relevant to another when the one is connected with the other in any of the ways referred to in the provisions of this Act relating to the relevancy of facts.


The expression "facts in issue" means and includes any fact from which, either by itself or in connection with other facts, the existence, non-existence, nature or extent of any right, liability, or disability, asserted or denied in any suit or proceeding, necessarily follows.


Explanation: Whenever, under the provisions of the law for the time being in force relating to Civil Procedure, any Court records an issue of fact, the fact to be asserted or denied in the answer to such issue is a fact in issue.


Illustrations:


A is accused of the murder of B.


At his trial the following facts may be in issue:-


that A caused B's death;


that A intended to cause B's death;


that A had received grave and sudden provocation from B;


that A, at the time of doing the act which caused B's death, was, by reason of unsoundness of mind, incapable of knowing its nature.


"Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter and includes any digital record.


Illustrations:


A writing is a document.


Words printed, lithographed or photographed are documents.


A map or plan is a document.


An inscription on a metal plate or stone is a document.


A caricature is a document.


"Evidence" means and includes:-


(1) all statements which the Court permits or requires to be made before it by witnesses, in relation to matters of fact under inquiry: such statements are called oral evidence;


(2) all documents produced for the inspection of the Court; such documents are called documentary evidence.


A fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist, or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it exists.


A fact is said to be disproved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes that it does not exist, or considers its non-existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it does not exist.


A fact is said not to be proved when it is neither proved nor disproved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a person's general reputation qualify as a 'fact' under the Evidence Act?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The legal definition of a fact encompasses physical objects as well as mental conditions and reputations.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Under Section 3, Illustration (e), the status of having a certain reputation is explicitly defined as a 'fact,' regardless of whether it is physical or mental.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, Illustration (e), the fact that a man has a certain reputation is legally considered a 'fact.'

Question: Scenario-based: A person was just sentenced to death by a local Sessions Court. According to the law, how many days do they have to file an appeal against this sentence?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 150
Possible Sections.Full Text: Under the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, from a sentence of death passed by a Court to Session or by the High Court Division in the exercise of its original Criminal Jurisdiction. Seven days

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 150

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for appealing a death sentence passed by a Court of Session?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The law provides an extremely short window of seven days to file an appeal against a death sentence.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the sentence involved is death, Article 150 applies specifically. The person must act within seven days of the sentence being passed to ensure their appeal is heard by the higher court.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 150, the person must file their appeal within seven days of the death sentence being passed by the Court of Session.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 150

Question: I lost the original contract for my house. Can I testify in court about what was written in that contract, or does the court only want to see the paper itself?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Oral admissions as to the contents of a document are not relevant, unless and until that the party proposing to prove them shows that he is entitled to give secondary evidence of the contents of such document under the rules hereinafter contained, or unless the genuineness of a document produced is in question.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When is oral testimony about the contents of a document admissible as an admission?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Oral statements about what a document contains are generally irrelevant unless the party first proves they have a legal right to provide secondary evidence.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the client lost the document, they must first satisfy the court they are 'entitled to give secondary evidence' before Section 22 allows their oral testimony.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 22, you cannot testify about what the document said unless you first prove to the court that you are legally allowed to use secondary evidence.

Question: Scenario-based: A man failed to sue to get his stolen jewelry back within the legal time limit of three years. He now wants to know if he still technically 'owns' the jewelry even if he can't sue for it.

Possible Sections.Full Text:
At the determination of the period hereby limited to any person for instituting a suit for possession of any property, his right to such property shall be extinguished.
Part V
SAVINGS AND REPEALS

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the expiration of the limitation period for a possession suit result in the loss of the underlying property right?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Once the time limit for suing to recover possession of property ends, the person's legal right to that property is completely terminated.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the three-year limit for the jewelry suit has passed, Section 28 operates to not only block the lawsuit but to permanently end the man's legal ownership of the items.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 28, the man's right to the jewelry is now completely extinguished — he no longer legally owns it because his time to sue for possession has run out.

Question: A suspect confessed after being threatened, but two days later, after the police told him he was safe and the threat was over, he confessed again. Is this second confession allowed in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: If such a confession as is referred to in section 24 is made after the impression caused by any such inducement, threat or promise has, in the opinion of the Court, been fully removed, it is relevant.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confession relevant if the initial illegal inducement or threat has been completely neutralized before the statement is made?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A confession becomes relevant if the court believes the negative impression from a prior threat or promise was fully removed before the confession was given.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the threat was neutralized and the 'impression' was removed before the second confession, it becomes relevant under Section 28.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 28, the second confession is relevant because it was made after the threat that caused the first confession had been fully removed.

Question: Scenario-based: A man who is suffering from a severe mental illness kills someone because he truly believes he is fighting an evil spirit. Can he be punished for murder?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does unsoundness of mind provide a complete defense against criminal liability if the person did not understand the nature of their act?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A person is exempt from criminal punishment if mental illness rendered them unable to understand that their action was wrong or illegal.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Due to his delusion, the man could not distinguish right from wrong at the time of the act, triggering the mental unsoundness exception.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 84, the man cannot be punished because his mental state made him incapable of knowing that his act was either wrong or illegal.

Question: Scenario-based: A 10-year-old child takes a gold ring from a neighbor's house and hides it. The child doesn't seem to understand that stealing is wrong. Can he be punished for theft?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child above nine years of age and under twelve, who has not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge of the nature and consequences of his conduct on that occasion.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a child between nine and twelve liable for a crime if they lack mature understanding of their actions?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A child in this age bracket is exempt from liability if they do not possess the mental maturity to understand the consequences of their act.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the 10-year-old child did not understand that his conduct was wrong, he is protected by the exception for immature understanding.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 83, the child cannot be punished because he is under twelve and has not attained sufficient maturity to understand the nature of his conduct.

Question: Scenario-based: A Session Judge sentences a man to death. Is the government allowed to hang the man immediately after the sentence is read in the local court?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 374
Possible Sections.Full Text: When the Court of Session passes sentence of death, the proceedings shall be submitted to the High Court Division and the sentence shall not be executed unless it is confirmed by the High Court Division.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 374

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a death sentence from a lower court immediately enforceable?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A death sentence passed by a Court of Session must be submitted to and confirmed by the High Court Division before it can be legally executed.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Section 374 mandates a mandatory review process; the man cannot be executed until the High Court Division formally reviews the records and confirms the penalty.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 374, the man cannot be executed right away; the death sentence must first be confirmed by the High Court Division.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 374
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A family mortgaged their home sixty years ago. They now have the money to pay it off and want the house back from the lender. Is it too late to redeem their mortgage?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 148
Possible Sections.Full Text: Against a mortgagee to redeem or to recover possession of immoveable property mortgaged. Sixty years.. When the right to redeem or to recover possession accrues.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 148

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a mortgagor to redeem their property from a mortgagee?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The right to redeem a mortgage or recover possession of mortgaged property expires sixty years after the right to redeem first accrued.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The mortgage was created sixty years ago. Under Article 148, the family is at the very end of their limitation period. If the sixty-year limit has already passed, their right to redeem is barred.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 148, the family has sixty years to redeem their mortgage — if sixty years have already passed since they could first pay it off, they are too late.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 148

Question: Scenario-based: Jamil wants to kill Rahim. He first hits Rahim with a heavy stick and then intentionally stops giving him the life-saving medicine Rahim needs. If Rahim dies from both, is Jamil guilty of murder?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Wherever the causing of a certain effect, or an attempt to cause that effect, by an act or by an omission, is an offence, it is to be understood that the causing of that effect partly by an act and partly by an omission is the same offence.


Illustration:


A intentionally causes Z's death, partly by illegally omitting to give Z food, and partly by beating Z. A has committed murder.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an offense committed if the result is caused by a combination of a physical act and an illegal failure to act?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: If causing an effect is an offense, achieving it through both an act and an omission constitutes that same offense.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Jamil caused death through both the physical blow and the omission of medicine; therefore, he is guilty of murder as per the combined effect.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 36, Jamil is guilty of murder because the law treats a crime caused by both an act and an omission as the same offense.

Question: Scenario-based: Salim tells his friend to commit an assault. The friend goes and commits the assault, but also decides to rob the victim at the same time. Is Salim liable for both the assault and the robbery?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
If the act for which the abettor is liable under the last preceding section is committed in addition to the act abetted, and constitutes a distinct offence, the abettor is liable to punishment for each of the offences.


Illustration:
A instigates B to resist by force a distress made by a public servant. B, in consequence, resists that distress. In offering the resistance, B voluntarily causes grievous hurt to the officer executing the distress. As B has committed both the offence of resisting the distress, and the offence of voluntarily causing grievous hurt, B is liable to punishment for both these offences; and, if A knew that B was likely voluntarily to cause grievous hurt in resisting the distress, A will also be liable to punishment for each of the offences.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an abettor responsible for multiple distinct offenses committed by the principal during the planned crime?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: If a perpetrator commits a distinct offense in addition to the one abetted, the abettor is liable for each crime they are responsible for.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the robbery was a separate act committed alongside the assault, Salim faces potential liability for both under the cumulative punishment rule.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 112, Salim may be liable for both offenses if the acts were committed together and are considered distinct crimes.

Question: Scenario-based: A man signs his name on the back of a bill of exchange. Does this signature count as a 'valuable security' under the law?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
The words "valuable security" denote a document which is, or purports to be, a document whereby any legal right is created, extended, transferred, restricted, extinguished or released, or whereby any person acknowledges that he lies under legal liability, or has not a certain legal right.


Illustration:


A writes his name on the back of a bill of exchange. As the effect of this endorsement is to transfer the right to the bill to any person who may become the lawful holder of it, the endorsement is a "valuable security".

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does an endorsement on a financial document qualify as a valuable security?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Any document that creates or transfers a legal right is classified as a valuable security under the Code.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since signing the back of the bill transfers the right to payment, the signature acts as a valuable security as defined by the statute.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 30, the signature is a valuable security because it is a document that transfers a legal right to another person.

Question: Scenario-based: A wants to cancel (rescind) a contract because of a mistake. Can the court do this if it's impossible to put the other person back in their original position?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Rescission of a contract in writing cannot be adjudged for mere mistake, unless the party against whom it is adjudged can be restored to substantially the same position as if the contract had not been made.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a court grant rescission for a mistake if the defendant cannot be returned to their pre-contractual status?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Rescission for a mistake is only allowed if the parties can be restored to their original positions.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Under Section 36, if the other person's position has changed so much that it can't be reversed, the court must refuse to cancel the contract for a 'mere mistake'.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 36, the court cannot cancel the contract for a mistake unless the other person can be put back in the same position they were in before the deal.

Question: Scenario-based: Two people signed a contract to sell a plot of land by a specific date, but the seller now refuses to go through with it. How long does the buyer have to sue to make the seller complete the sale?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 113
Possible Sections.Full Text: For specific performance of a contract. 1 [One year] The date fixed for the performance, or, if no such date is fixed, when the plaintiff has notice that performance is refused.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 113

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for filing a suit for specific performance of a contract?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A suit to enforce a contract must be filed within one year of the performance date or the date refusal was communicated.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the contract had a specific date for the land sale, the buyer's one-year limit begins on that date. If no date was set, it would start when the seller officially refused.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 113, the buyer has exactly one year to sue for the sale — the time starts from the date that was fixed in the contract for performance.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 113

Question: Scenario-based: Two jailors, A and B, take turns guarding a prisoner. They both agree to starve him to death. A refuses food during his 6-hour shift, and B does the same during his. Who is guilty of murder?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
When an offence is committed by means of several acts, whoever intentionally co-operates in the commission of that offence by doing any one of those acts, either singly or jointly with any other person, commits that offence.


Illustrations:


(a) A and B agree to murder Z by severally and at different times giving him small doses of poison. A and B administer the poison according to the agreement with intent to murder Z. Z dies from the effects of the several doses of poison so administered to him. Here A and B intentionally co-operate in the commission of murder and as each of them does an act by which the death is caused, they are both guilty of the offence though their acts are separate.


(b) A and B are joint jailors, and as such, have the charge of Z, a prisoner, alternately for six hours at a time. A and B, intending to cause Z's death, knowingly co-operate in causing that effect by illegally omitting, each during the time of his attendance, to furnish Z with food supplied to them for that purpose. Z dies of hunger. Both A and B are guilty of the murder of Z.


(c) A, a jailor, has the charge of Z, a prisoner. A intending to cause Z's death, illegally omits to supply Z with food; in consequence of which Z is much reduced in strength, but the starvation is not sufficient to cause his death. A is dismissed from his office, and B succeeds him. B, without collusion or co-operation with A, illegally omits to supply Z with food, knowing that he is likely thereby to cause Z's death. Z dies of hunger. B is guilty of murder, but, as A did not co-operate with B, A is guilty only of an attempt to commit murder.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are individuals liable for murder if they cooperatively perform separate acts that lead to a single death?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Intentional cooperation in an offense through several distinct acts makes each participant guilty of the final crime.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: A and B worked together through separate omissions to achieve a common fatal goal, making both legally responsible for the resulting murder.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 37, Illustration (b), both jailors are guilty of murder because they intentionally cooperated to cause the prisoner's death through their separate actions.

Question: Scenario-based: A man is caught using a machine to make very high-quality fake 500-Taka coins. What is the most severe prison sentence he could face for this crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting Bangladesh coin, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the maximum penalty for counterfeiting national currency?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The act of counterfeiting Bangladesh coins is a serious offense punishable by life imprisonment or a ten-year term, plus a fine.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: By making fake Taka coins, the man has performed the process of counterfeiting Bangladesh coin, making him eligible for life imprisonment under Section 232.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 232, the man could face up to life imprisonment or ten years in prison, and he will also have to pay a fine.

Question: Scenario-based: A buyer wants to force a seller to perform only the first half of a large contract because the second half is illegal. Can the court order this part performance if it doesn't fall under the specific exceptions for small or independent parts?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The Court shall not direct the specific performance of a part of a contract except in cases coming under one or other of the three last preceding sections.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a court grant specific performance for only a portion of a contract outside of the statutory exceptions?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Partial specific performance is strictly prohibited unless the case qualifies under sections 14, 15, or 16 of the Act.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the buyer's request does not fit the specific criteria of sections 14-16, Section 17 acts as a mandatory bar against enforcing only part of the contract.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 17, the court cannot order part performance of the contract unless the situation specifically fits the narrow exceptions listed in the preceding sections.

Question: Scenario-based: I ordered a new TV, and it passed through three different delivery drivers before reaching me. Is the fact that the first driver picked it up from the warehouse relevant to proving I received it?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.


Illustrations:


(a) A is accused of the murder of B by beating him. Whatever was said or done by A or B or the bystanders at the beating, or so shortly before or after it as to form part of the transaction, is a relevant fact.


(b) A is accused of waging war against Bangladesh by taking part in an armed insurrection in which property is destroyed, troops are attacked, and jails are broken open. The occurrence of these facts is relevant, as forming part of the general transaction, though A may not have been present at all of them.


(c) A sues B for a libel contained in a letter forming part of a correspondence. Letters between the parties relating to the subject out of which the libel arose, and forming part of the correspondence in which it is contained, are relevant facts, though they do not contain the libel itself.


(d) The question is whether certain goods ordered from B were delivered to A. The goods were delivered to several intermediate persons successively. Each delivery is a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is each step in a chain of delivery relevant to proving the final receipt of goods?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: In a transaction involving multiple successive steps, every individual part of that transaction is considered a relevant fact.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Proving the final delivery requires proving the chain; thus, the first driver's pickup is a relevant fact under the transaction rule of Section 6, Illustration (d).
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 6, Illustration (d), the first driver's pickup is a relevant fact because every intermediate step in the delivery process is part of the same transaction.

Question: Scenario-based: A leader stands on a stage and tells a group of 20 followers to go and burn down a nearby store. What is the longest prison sentence the leader can receive for this act of public instigation?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Whoever abets the commission of an offence by the public generally or by any number or class of persons exceeding ten, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.


Illustration:
A affixes in a public place a placard instigating a sect consisting of more than ten members to meet at a certain time and place, for the purpose of attacking the members of an adverse sect, while engaged in a procession. A has committed the offence defined in this section.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the maximum penalty for abetting a crime when the target audience exceeds ten people?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Abetting an offense by the public or any group larger than ten individuals is punishable by up to three years in prison.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the leader instigated twenty people, he meets the numerical threshold for enhanced punishment, capping his jail time at three years.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 117, the leader can be punished with a maximum of three years in prison, a fine, or both, for abetting more than ten people.

Question: How does a judge decide if a piece of information is 'proved' during my trial? Is it based on a gut feeling or a specific standard?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:-


"Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence.


"Fact" means and includes:-


(1) anything, state of things, or relation of things capable of being perceived by the senses;


(2) any mental condition of which any person is conscious.


Illustrations:


(a) That there are certain objects arranged in a certain order in a certain place, is a fact.


(b) That a man heard or saw something, is a fact.


(c) That a man said certain words, is a fact.


(d) That a man holds a certain opinion, has a certain intention, acts in good faith or fraudulently, or uses a particular word in a particular sense, or is or was at a specified time conscious of a particular sensation, is a fact.


(e) That a man has a certain reputation, is a fact.


One fact is said to be relevant to another when the one is connected with the other in any of the ways referred to in the provisions of this Act relating to the relevancy of facts.


The expression "facts in issue" means and includes any fact from which, either by itself or in connection with other facts, the existence, non-existence, nature or extent of any right, liability, or disability, asserted or denied in any suit or proceeding, necessarily follows.


Explanation: Whenever, under the provisions of the law for the time being in force relating to Civil Procedure, any Court records an issue of fact, the fact to be asserted or denied in the answer to such issue is a fact in issue.


Illustrations:


A is accused of the murder of B.


At his trial the following facts may be in issue:-


that A caused B's death;


that A intended to cause B's death;


that A had received grave and sudden provocation from B;


that A, at the time of doing the act which caused B's death, was, by reason of unsoundness of mind, incapable of knowing its nature.


"Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter and includes any digital record.


Illustrations:


A writing is a document.


Words printed, lithographed or photographed are documents.


A map or plan is a document.


An inscription on a metal plate or stone is a document.


A caricature is a document.


"Evidence" means and includes:-


(1) all statements which the Court permits or requires to be made before it by witnesses, in relation to matters of fact under inquiry: such statements are called oral evidence;


(2) all documents produced for the inspection of the Court; such documents are called documentary evidence.


A fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist, or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it exists.


A fact is said to be disproved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes that it does not exist, or considers its non-existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it does not exist.


A fact is said not to be proved when it is neither proved nor disproved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the legal standard for a court to declare a fact 'proved'?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A fact is proved if the court believes it exists or finds its existence so probable that a reasonable person would assume its truth.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Judges apply the 'prudent man' standard under Section 3 to weigh probabilities before concluding that a fact is legally established.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, a fact is 'proved' when the court considers its existence so probable that a reasonable person would act as if it exists.

Question: Scenario-based: A man convinces a 6-year-old child to steal a piece of jewelry for him. Is the man guilty of a crime even though the child cannot be punished by law?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
A person abets an offence, who abets either the commission of an offence, or the commission of an act which would be an offence, if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence with the same intention or knowledge as that of the abettor.


Explanation 1.-The abetment of the illegal omission of an act may amount to an offence although the abettor may not himself be bound to do that act.


Explanation 2.-To constitute the offence of abetment it is not necessary that the act abetted should be committed, or that the effect requisite to constitute the offence should be caused.


Illustrations:
(a) A instigates B to murder C. B refuses to do so. A is guilty of abetting B to commit murder.
(b) A instigates B to murder D. B in pursuance of the instigation stabs D. D recovers from the wound. A is guilty of instigating B to commit murder.


Explanation 3.-It is not necessary that the person abetted should be capable by law of committing an offence, or that he should have the same guilty intention or knowledge as that of the abettor, or any guilty intention or knowledge.


Illustrations:
(a) A, with a guilty intention, abets a child or a lunatic to commit an act which would be an offence, if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence, and having the same intention as A. Here A, whether the act be committed or not, is guilty of abetting an offence.
(b) A, with the intention of murdering Z, instigates B, a child under seven years of age, to do an act which causes Z's death. B, in consequence of the abetment, does the act in the absence of A and thereby, causes Z's death. Here, though B was not capable by law of committing an offence, A is liable to be punished in the same manner as if B had been capable by law of committing an offence, and had committed murder, and he is therefore subject to the punishment of death.
(c) A instigates B to set fire to a dwelling-house. B, in consequence of the unsoundness of his mind, being incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is wrong or contrary to law, sets fire to the house in consequence of A's instigation. B has committed no offence, but A is guilty of abetting the offence of setting fire to a dwelling-house, and is liable to the punishment provided for that offence.
(d) A intending to cause a theft to be committed, instigates B to take property belonging to Z out of Z's possession. A induces B to believe that the property belongs to A. B takes the property out of Z's possession, in good faith, believing it to be A's property. B, acting under this misconception, does not take dishonestly, and therefore does not commit theft. But A is guilty of abetting theft, and is liable to the same punishment as if B had committed theft.


Explanation 4.-The abetment of an offence being an offence, the abetment of such an abetment is also an offence.


Illustration:
A instigates B to instigate C to murder Z. B accordingly instigates C to murder Z, and C commits that offence in consequence of B's instigation. B is liable to be punished for his offence with the punishment for murder; and, as A instigated B to commit the offence, A is also liable to the same punishment.


Explanation 5.-It is not necessary to the commission of the offence of abetment by conspiracy that the abettor should concert the offence with the person who commits it. It is sufficient if he engages in the conspiracy in pursuance of which the offence is committed.


Illustration:
A concerts with B a plan for poisoning Z. It is agreed that A shall administer the poison. B then explains the plan to C mentioning that a third person is to administer the poison, but without mentioning A's name. C agrees to procure the poison, and procures and delivers it to B for the purpose of its being used in the manner explained. A administers the poison; Z dies in consequence. Here, though A and C have not conspired together, yet C has been engaged in the conspiracy in pursuance of which Z has been murdered. C has therefore committed the offence defined in this section and is liable to the punishment for murder.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person liable for abetment if they use an individual who is legally incapable of committing a crime to perform the act?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Abetment is established even if the person performing the act is legally immune due to age or mental status.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The man is liable for the theft because the law treats the instigator as the principal criminal when using an incapable child.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 108, Explanation 3, the man is guilty of abetment even if the child is not legally capable of committing the crime themselves.

Question: Scenario-based: A health inspector is trying to check a restaurant for safety. The owner physically stands in the way and refuses to let him enter. Has the owner committed a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any public servant in the discharge of his public functions, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does blocking a public servant from performing their official duties constitute a criminal act?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Voluntary obstruction of a public servant who is carrying out their official functions is a punishable offense.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: By physically blocking the health inspector's entry, the restaurant owner has voluntarily obstructed a public function, triggering Section 186.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 186, the owner is guilty because he voluntarily obstructed a public servant who was trying to perform his official duties.

Question: Scenario-based: A father believes his child is being kept hidden in a private house against his will by a distant relative. Can the father ask the High Court to intervene and bring the child to court?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 491(1)(b)

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 491(1)(b)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is the High Court Division authorized to order the release of a person held in private, illegal detention?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The High Court may issue directions to set free any individual found to be improperly or illegally detained in any public or private place.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the child is being held against his will in a private house, Section 491(1)(b) allows the High Court to order that the child be brought before it and set free.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 491, Subsection (1), Clause (b), the High Court Division can order that the child be brought to court and set free from illegal private detention.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 491(1)(b)

Question: If I am accused of using a fake coin, can I prove that I actually showed the coin to a expert earlier because I wasn't sure if it was real?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they cannot be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–


(1) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, when it is of such a nature that, if the person making it were dead, it would be relevant as between third persons under section 32.


(2) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, when it consists of a statement of the existence of any state of mind or body, relevant or in issue, made at or about the time when such state of mind or body existed, and is accompanied by conduct rendering its falsehood improbable.


(3) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, if it is relevant otherwise than as an admission.


Illustrations:


(a) The question between A and B is, whether a certain deed is or is not forged. A affirms that it is genuine, B that it is forged.


A may prove a statement by B that the deed is genuine, and B may prove a statement by A that the deed is forged; but A cannot prove a statement by himself that the deed is genuine, nor can B prove a statement by himself that the deed is forged.


(b) A, the captain of a ship, is tried for casting her away.


Evidence is given to show that the ship was taken out of her proper course.


A produces a book kept by him in the ordinary course of his business showing observations alleged to have been taken by him from day to day, and indicating that the ship was not taken out of her proper course. A may prove these statements, because they would be admissible between third parties, if he were dead, under section 32, clause (2).


(c) A is accused of a crime committed by him at Chittagong.


He produces a letter written by himself and dated at Dhaka on that day and bearing the Dhaka post-mark of that day.


The statement in the date of the letter is admissible, because, if A were dead it would be admissible under section 32, clause (2).


(d) A is accused of receiving stolen goods knowing them to be stolen.


He offers to prove that he refused to sell them below their value.


A may prove these statements, though they are admissions, because they are explanatory of conduct influenced by facts in issue.


(e) A is accused of fraudulently having in his possession counterfeit coin which he knew to be counterfeit.


He offers to prove that he asked a skilful person to examine the coin as he doubted whether it was counterfeit or not, and that that person did examine it and told him it was genuine.


A may prove these facts for the reasons stated in the last preceding illustration.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person's act of seeking an expert opinion on potentially illegal property relevant to their own defense?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Self-serving statements are admissible if they explain a party's conduct in relation to the primary facts of the case.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Asking an expert to examine the coin explains the accused's 'good faith' conduct; thus, Section 21, Illustration (e) allows them to prove this fact in their favor.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 21, Illustration (e), you are allowed to prove that you asked an expert to check the coin, as it explains your cautious conduct.

Question: Scenario-based: An army officer is accused of deserting his post. His lawyer argues that the general Penal Code should apply instead of military laws. Is this argument legally valid?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing in this Act is intended to repeal, vary, suspend, or affect 7[* * *] any of the provisions of any Act for punishing mutiny and desertion of officers, soldiers, sailors or airmen in the service of the 8[Republic], or of any special or local law.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the Penal Code override specific military laws regarding desertion and mutiny?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The Code does not repeal or affect existing laws designed for punishing military desertion or special local regulations.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the officer's act falls under military desertion, Section 5 expressly preserves the authority of the relevant military Act over the general Code.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 5, the argument is invalid because the Code specifically states it does not affect any Act for punishing military desertion or any special or local law.

Question: Scenario-based: A known habitual thief is brought before a magistrate. What is the longest amount of time the magistrate can order him to stay on good behavior under a security bond?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 110
Possible Sections.Full Text:
Whenever a District Magistrate, or any other Executive Magistrate specially empowered in this behalf by the Government receives information that any person within the local limits of his jurisdiction-


    (a) is by habit a robber, house-breaker, thief, or forger, or


    (b) is by habit a receiver of stolen property knowing the same to have been stolen, or


    (c) habitually protects or harbours thieves or aids, in the concealment or disposal of stolen property, or


    (d) habitually commits, or attempts to commit, or abets the commission of, the offence of kidnapping, abduction, extortion, cheating or mischief, or any offence punishable under Chapter XII of the Penal Code, or under section 489A, section 489B, section 489C or section 489D of that Code, or


    (e) habitually commits, or attempts to commit, or abets the commission of, offences involving a breach of the peace, or


    (f) is so desperate and dangerous as to render his being at large without security hazardous to the community,


    such Magistrate may, in manner hereinafter provided, require such person to show cause why he should not be ordered to execute a bond, with sureties, for his good behaviour for such period, not exceeding three years, as the Magistrate thinks fit to fix.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 110

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the maximum statutory duration for a good-behavior bond for a habitual offender?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Magistrates may require a security bond for good behavior from habitual criminals for a term that cannot exceed three years.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: By applying Section 110, the magistrate is legally constrained to a three-year ceiling when determining the length of the good-behavior period for the thief.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 110, the magistrate can order the habitual thief to execute a bond for good behavior for a period of up to three years.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 110

Question: Scenario-based: I am suing someone for a car accident. Is the fact that the other driver was speeding considered a 'fact in issue' for the case?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:-


"Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence.
