A fact is said not to be proved when it is neither proved nor disproved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the speed of a driver constitute a 'fact in issue' in a liability suit?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A fact in issue is any fact from which a legal liability or right asserted in a case necessarily follows.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: In an accident suit, speeding directly determines the 'extent of liability' and negligence; thus, it is a central fact in issue under Section 3.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, the speed of the driver is a 'fact in issue' because it directly relates to the existence of their legal liability for the accident.

Question: Scenario-based: A judge has already signed a final judgment in a case. Later that day, he realizes he made a small spelling error in the defendant's name. Can he still fix it?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 369
Possible Sections.Full Text: Save as otherwise provided by this Code or by any other law for the time being in force, no Court when it has signed its judgment, shall alter or review the same, except to correct a clerical error.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 369

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a judge change their mind and rewrite a final judgment after they have already signed the document?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Once a judgment is signed, the court is strictly forbidden from altering or reviewing it, except to fix minor clerical mistakes.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The judge's attempt to fix a spelling error is legal; Section 369 allows post-signature changes specifically for small clerical or typing mistakes.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 369, a court cannot change its final judgment after signing it, except to correct small clerical or typing mistakes.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 369
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A person is arrested for stealing items from inside someone's home. Does the suspect have a right to be let out on bail while waiting for trial?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 380
Possible Sections.Full Text: [Repealed by section 16 of the Probation of Offenders Ordinance, 1960 (Ordinance No. XLV of 1960).]

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 380

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is the offence of theft inside a building considered bailable under the legal schedule?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The act of theft within a residential building is specifically categorized as a non-bailable offence in the procedural schedule.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the 'Bailable' column for Section 380 IPC in Schedule II states 'Not bailable,' the suspect cannot claim bail as a right and must remain in custody.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 380, the person cannot be released on bail because theft in a building is a non-bailable offence.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 380
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: I have a series of WhatsApp messages from a contractor promising to finish my house. Can these digital messages be submitted as 'documents' in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:-


"Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence.


"Fact" means and includes:-


(1) anything, state of things, or relation of things capable of being perceived by the senses;


(2) any mental condition of which any person is conscious.


Illustrations:


(a) That there are certain objects arranged in a certain order in a certain place, is a fact.


(b) That a man heard or saw something, is a fact.


(c) That a man said certain words, is a fact.


(d) That a man holds a certain opinion, has a certain intention, acts in good faith or fraudulently, or uses a particular word in a particular sense, or is or was at a specified time conscious of a particular sensation, is a fact.


(e) That a man has a certain reputation, is a fact.


One fact is said to be relevant to another when the one is connected with the other in any of the ways referred to in the provisions of this Act relating to the relevancy of facts.


The expression "facts in issue" means and includes any fact from which, either by itself or in connection with other facts, the existence, non-existence, nature or extent of any right, liability, or disability, asserted or denied in any suit or proceeding, necessarily follows.


Explanation: Whenever, under the provisions of the law for the time being in force relating to Civil Procedure, any Court records an issue of fact, the fact to be asserted or denied in the answer to such issue is a fact in issue.


Illustrations:


A is accused of the murder of B.


At his trial the following facts may be in issue:-


that A caused B's death;


that A intended to cause B's death;


that A had received grave and sudden provocation from B;


that A, at the time of doing the act which caused B's death, was, by reason of unsoundness of mind, incapable of knowing its nature.


"Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter and includes any digital record.


Illustrations:


A writing is a document.


Words printed, lithographed or photographed are documents.


A map or plan is a document.


An inscription on a metal plate or stone is a document.


A caricature is a document.


"Evidence" means and includes:-


(1) all statements which the Court permits or requires to be made before it by witnesses, in relation to matters of fact under inquiry: such statements are called oral evidence;


(2) all documents produced for the inspection of the Court; such documents are called documentary evidence.


A fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist, or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it exists.


A fact is said to be disproved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes that it does not exist, or considers its non-existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it does not exist.


A fact is said not to be proved when it is neither proved nor disproved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Do digital text messages meet the statutory definition of a 'document'?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The definition of a document includes any matter recorded through letters or marks and specifically incorporates all digital records.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since WhatsApp messages are digital records used to record a promise, they fall under the expanded definition of a document in Section 3.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, digital messages are considered 'documents.' The law expressly includes digital records within its definition of documentary evidence.

Question: Scenario-based: There is a court judgment that says a certain piece of land is a public road. Can I use this judgment in my own private land dispute to show the land's public nature?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Judgments, orders or decrees other than those mentioned in section 41 are relevant if they relate to matters of a public nature relevant to the inquiry; but such judgments, orders or decrees are not conclusive proof of that which they state.


Illustration


A sues B for trespass on his land. B alleges the existence of a public right of way over the land, which A denies.


The existence of a decree in favour of the defendant, in a suit by A against C for a trespass on the same land, in which C alleged the existence of the same right of way, is relevant, but it is not conclusive proof that the right of way exists.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a prior judgment about a public matter relevant in a private dispute involving that same matter?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Judgments relating to matters of a public nature are relevant, though they do not constitute conclusive proof of the facts they state.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The previous ruling on the 'public road' relates to a public nature relevant to the current enquiry; thus, Section 42 declares the judgment relevant but not final.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 42, the judgment is relevant because it relates to a matter of a public nature, though it is not conclusive proof.

Question: Scenario-based: The plaintiff arrives for the trial, but the defendant is missing. The court sees proof that the defendant was personally served the notice weeks ago. Can the judge decide the case right then?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 6(1)(a)
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where the plaintiff appears and the defendant does not appear when the suit is called on for hearing, then— (a) if it is proved that the summons was duly served, the Court may proceed ex parte.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 6(1)(a)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: May a court legally conduct a trial and issue a judgment when a served defendant fails to appear?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The court possesses the authority to hear a case ex parte if the plaintiff is present and service is verified.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the defendant was served but failed to attend, Rule 6(1)(a) allows the judge to proceed with the hearing immediately.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 6, Sub-rule (1)(a), the court may proceed to hear the case ex parte, provided it is proven that the defendant was properly served with the summons.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 6(1)(a)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Single hop

Question: A man says his house burned down by accident. Can the insurance company show that his last three houses also burned down to prove it wasn't an accident?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.


Illustrations:


(a) A is accused of burning down his house in order to obtain money for which it is insured.


The facts that A lived in several houses successively each of which he insured, in each of which a fire occurred, and after each of which fires A received payment from a different insurance office, are relevant, as tending to show that the fires were not accidental.


(b) A is employed to receive money from the debtors of B. It is A's duty to make entries in a book showing the amounts received by him. He makes an entry showing that on a particular occasion he received less than he really did receive.


The question is, whether this false entry was accidental or intentional.


The facts that other entries made by A in the same book are false, and that the false entry is in each case in favour of A, are relevant.


(c) A is accused of fraudulently delivering to B a counterfeit Taka.


The question is, whether the delivery of the Taka was accidental.


The facts that, soon before or soon after the delivery to B, A delivered counterfeit Taka to C, D and E are relevant, as showing that the delivery to B was not accidental.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are prior similar occurrences relevant to deciding if an act was intentional or accidental?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: If an act belongs to a series of similar events involving the same person, those events are relevant to prove intent.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The previous fires form a 'series of similar occurrences' which Section 15 makes relevant to disprove the man's claim that the current fire was accidental.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 15, Illustration (a), the previous house fires are relevant because they suggest the current fire was intentional rather than an accident.

Question: Scenario-based: A full-time government teacher is legally forbidden by his contract and law from having another job. However, he secretly runs a hardware shop in the evenings. Is this act a crime under the Penal Code?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, being a public servant, and being legally bound as such public servant not to engage in trade, engages in trade, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a government employee be punished for running a private business if they are legally forbidden from doing so?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Public servants who are legally bound not to engage in trade but do so anyway face up to one year of imprisonment.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the teacher is restricted from private commercial activity by his legal duty, his secret ownership of a shop constitutes a violation of Section 168.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 168, the teacher has committed a crime because he engaged in trade while being legally bound as a public servant not to do so.

Question: Scenario-based: A businessman brings a box of fake foreign coins into Bangladesh, knowing they are counterfeit, hoping to sell them to collectors. Is this act of bringing them in a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever imports into Bangladesh, or exports therefrom, any counterfeit coin, knowingly or having reason to believe that the same is counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is it illegal to import counterfeit coins into the country if you know they are fake?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The law prohibits the import or export of any counterfeit coin when the person knows or believes it to be fake.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The businessman's act of bringing known fake coins across the border constitutes illegal importation, fulfilling the elements of Section 237.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 237, the businessman has committed a crime because it is illegal to import any counterfeit coin that you know is fake.

Question: Scenario-based: Someone was recently kicked out of their house without any legal reason and wants to get it back through a summary suit. From the day they were forced out, how long do they have to file a case?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 3
Possible Sections.Full Text: Under the Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 9, to recover possession of immoveable property. Part III.—Six months. Six months . . When the dispossession occurs.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 3

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the statutory limitation period for a suit to recover possession under the Specific Relief Act after dispossession?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A party seeking to regain property after being dispossessed must file their claim within a six-month window from the event.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The law triggers a six-month countdown starting exactly when the person was removed from the house. Filing after this period would bar this specific summary remedy under Article 3.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 3, the person has exactly six months to file the case — this time limit begins counting from the very day they were forced out of the property.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 3

Question: Scenario-based: During a talk to end a lawsuit, I told the other side, 'I'll pay you Tk. 50,000 if we keep this secret.' Can they use that offer against me in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In civil cases no admission is relevant, if it is made either upon an express condition that evidence of it is not to be given, or under circumstances from which the Court can infer that the parties agreed together that evidence of it should not be given.


Explanation.– Nothing in this section shall be taken to exempt any Advocate from giving evidence of any matter of which he may be compelled to give evidence under section 126.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a confidential admission made during settlement negotiations admissible in a civil court?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Admissions in civil cases are irrelevant if they are made on the express condition that they will not be used as evidence in court.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The offer was made on the express condition of secrecy during a settlement talk; thus, Section 23 makes that admission irrelevant and inadmissible in court.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 23, no. Because the offer was made on the condition that it would stay secret, it cannot be used against you in a civil case.

Question: Scenario-based: A key witness is too sick to travel to court. Can the judge send an official to the witness's house to record their statement instead of making them come to the courtroom?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 75(a)

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 75(a)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a court have the authority to record witness testimony outside of the courtroom environment?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Courts may issue a 'Commission' to authorize an official to interview and examine a witness at another location.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the witness is physically unable to attend, the court can use its power under Clause (a) to send a commissioner to record the evidence at home.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 75, Clause (a), the court can issue a commission to have someone officially record the sick witness's statement at their house.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 75(a)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A woman was forced to sign a deed under threat. She finally escaped that situation today and learned the full details of what she signed. How long does she have to ask the court to cancel that document?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 91
Possible Sections.Full Text: To cancel or set aside an instrument not otherwise provided for. Three years.. When the facts entitling the plaintiff to have the instrument cancelled or set aside become known to him.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 91

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for cancelling a legal document and when does it begin?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A suit to cancel or set aside an instrument must be brought within three years of the plaintiff learning the facts that justify the cancellation.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The woman learned the relevant facts today upon her escape. Under Article 91, she now has a three-year window from today to file a lawsuit to have the deed officially cancelled.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 91, the woman has three years to sue to cancel the deed — the time limit starts today, as she now knows the facts of the situation.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 91

Question: Scenario-based: Kamal helps plan a robbery. On the night of the crime, he stands outside the house as the others go in. Can he be punished as a primary criminal or only as a 'helper' who wasn't inside?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whenever any person, who if absent would be liable to be punished as an abettor, is present when the act or offence for which he would be punishable in consequence of the abetment is committed, he shall be deemed to have committed such act or offence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an abettor treated as a principal offender if they are physically present at the scene of the crime?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Any person who would normally be an abettor is legally deemed to have committed the offense themselves if they are present during its commission.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Kamal's presence at the scene, even outside, triggers the legal presumption that he committed the robbery alongside the principal actors.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 114, Kamal is punished as if he committed the robbery himself because he was present at the scene during the crime.

Question: Scenario-based: A tenant has the right to use a piece of land for farming, but he instead built a small shop on it. The landlord just found out about this yesterday. How long does the landlord have to sue?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 32
Possible Sections.Full Text: Against one who, having a right to use property for specific purposes, perverts it to other purposes. Two years.. When the perversion first becomes known to the person injured thereby.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 32

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a suit against a person who misuses property for an unauthorized purpose?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A suit regarding the unauthorized use of property must be filed within two years of the owner discovering the misuse.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The landlord discovered the unauthorized shop yesterday. Under Article 32, this starts a two-year period during which the landlord must file a suit against the tenant for perverting the property's use.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 32, the landlord has two years to sue the tenant — the time starts counting from yesterday, when the landlord first discovered the shop.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 32

Question: Scenario-based: A local judge is confused about whether a new government regulation is legally valid. Can the judge ask the High Court for an explanation before finishing the trial?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 113
Possible Sections.Full Text: Subject to such conditions and limitations as may be prescribed, any Court may state a case and refer the same for the opinion of the High Court Division, and the High Court Division may make such order thereon as it thinks fit.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 113

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: May a subordinate court seek a legal opinion from a higher court regarding a complex point of law?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A lower court can refer a specific case or legal question to the High Court Division for a definitive opinion.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The judge's confusion regarding the regulation allows for a 'Reference' under Section 113 to obtain the High Court's binding guidance before proceeding.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 113, the local judge can send the legal question to the High Court Division for an official opinion on the law.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 113
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A woman is sentenced to death for a crime, but a medical report shows she is currently pregnant. What is the court required to do about her execution?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 382
Possible Sections.Full Text: If a woman sentenced to death is found to be pregnant, the High Court Division shall order the execution of the sentence to be postponed, and may, if it thinks fit, commute the sentence to transportation for life.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 382

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What must the High Court Division do if a woman who has been sentenced to death is found to be pregnant?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The court is required to postpone the execution of the death sentence and may choose to change the punishment to life imprisonment instead.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the woman is expecting a child, Section 382 mandates that the High Court stop the execution and gives them the option to commute her sentence.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 382, if a woman on death row is pregnant, the High Court must delay her execution and may change her sentence to life.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 382
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A sues for specific performance, but the court dismisses the case. Can A now file a new, separate case just to ask for money (compensation) for the same breach?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The dismissal of a suit for specific performance of a contract or part thereof shall bar the plaintiff's right to sue for compensation for the breach of such contract or part, as the case may be.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the dismissal of a specific performance suit prevent the plaintiff from later suing for monetary damages for the same breach?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Once a suit for specific performance is dismissed, the plaintiff is legally barred from filing a separate suit for compensation for the same breach.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Under Section 29, the dismissal of A's first suit acts as a permanent bar to any future claims for money related to that contract breach.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 29, A cannot file a new case for money because the dismissal of his specific performance suit bars him from asking for compensation later.

Question: Scenario-based: I am accused of receiving stolen goods. Can I tell the court that I refused to sell the goods for a very low price to show that I didn't know they were stolen?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they cannot be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–


(1) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, when it is of such a nature that, if the person making it were dead, it would be relevant as between third persons under section 32.


(2) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, when it consists of a statement of the existence of any state of mind or body, relevant or in issue, made at or about the time when such state of mind or body existed, and is accompanied by conduct rendering its falsehood improbable.


(3) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, if it is relevant otherwise than as an admission.


Illustrations:


(a) The question between A and B is, whether a certain deed is or is not forged. A affirms that it is genuine, B that it is forged.


A may prove a statement by B that the deed is genuine, and B may prove a statement by A that the deed is forged; but A cannot prove a statement by himself that the deed is genuine, nor can B prove a statement by himself that the deed is forged.


(b) A, the captain of a ship, is tried for casting her away.


Evidence is given to show that the ship was taken out of her proper course.


A produces a book kept by him in the ordinary course of his business showing observations alleged to have been taken by him from day to day, and indicating that the ship was not taken out of her proper course. A may prove these statements, because they would be admissible between third parties, if he were dead, under section 32, clause (2).


(c) A is accused of a crime committed by him at Chittagong.


He produces a letter written by himself and dated at Dhaka on that day and bearing the Dhaka post-mark of that day.


The statement in the date of the letter is admissible, because, if A were dead it would be admissible under section 32, clause (2).


(d) A is accused of receiving stolen goods knowing them to be stolen.


He offers to prove that he refused to sell them below their value.


A may prove these statements, though they are admissions, because they are explanatory of conduct influenced by facts in issue.


(e) A is accused of fraudulently having in his possession counterfeit coin which he knew to be counterfeit.


He offers to prove that he asked a skilful person to examine the coin as he doubted whether it was counterfeit or not, and that that person did examine it and told him it was genuine.


A may prove these facts for the reasons stated in the last preceding illustration.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can an accused person prove their own prior statement to explain their honest conduct?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A person can prove their own statements if those statements serve to explain conduct that was influenced by the main facts of the case.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Refusing a low sale price explains 'honest conduct'; therefore, Section 21, Illustration (d) permits the accused to prove their own statement in court.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 21, Illustration (d), yes, you can prove that you refused a low price because it helps explain your honest conduct regarding the goods.

Question: I am a ship captain accused of crashing my ship on purpose. Can I use my daily logbook to show that I was following the correct course the entire time?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they cannot be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–


(1) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, when it is of such a nature that, if the person making it were dead, it would be relevant as between third persons under section 32.


(2) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, when it consists of a statement of the existence of any state of mind or body, relevant or in issue, made at or about the time when such state of mind or body existed, and is accompanied by conduct rendering its falsehood improbable.


(3) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, if it is relevant otherwise than as an admission.


Illustrations:


(a) The question between A and B is, whether a certain deed is or is not forged. A affirms that it is genuine, B that it is forged.


A may prove a statement by B that the deed is genuine, and B may prove a statement by A that the deed is forged; but A cannot prove a statement by himself that the deed is genuine, nor can B prove a statement by himself that the deed is forged.


(b) A, the captain of a ship, is tried for casting her away.


Evidence is given to show that the ship was taken out of her proper course.


A produces a book kept by him in the ordinary course of his business showing observations alleged to have been taken by him from day to day, and indicating that the ship was not taken out of her proper course. A may prove these statements, because they would be admissible between third parties, if he were dead, under section 32, clause (2).


(c) A is accused of a crime committed by him at Chittagong.


He produces a letter written by himself and dated at Dhaka on that day and bearing the Dhaka post-mark of that day.


The statement in the date of the letter is admissible, because, if A were dead it would be admissible under section 32, clause (2).


(d) A is accused of receiving stolen goods knowing them to be stolen.


He offers to prove that he refused to sell them below their value.


A may prove these statements, though they are admissions, because they are explanatory of conduct influenced by facts in issue.


(e) A is accused of fraudulently having in his possession counterfeit coin which he knew to be counterfeit.


He offers to prove that he asked a skilful person to examine the coin as he doubted whether it was counterfeit or not, and that that person did examine it and told him it was genuine.


A may prove these facts for the reasons stated in the last preceding illustration.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a professional logbook kept by a party admissible for that party's own benefit?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A party can prove their own business record if that record would be admissible under the 'business entry' rules if they were deceased.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The captain's log is a business record; under Section 21 and Section 32(2), it is admissible for the captain's defense as Illustration (b) confirms.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 21, Illustration (b), yes, you can prove your logbook statements because they would be admissible if you were deceased under the business record rules.

Question: Scenario-based: Zahid is sentenced to 4 years in prison and a fine for an offense. If he refuses to pay the fine, what is the maximum extra time he can be kept in jail?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The term for which the Court directs the offender to be imprisoned in default of payment of a fine shall not exceed one-fourth of the term of imprisonment which is the maximum fixed for the offence, if the offence be punishable with imprisonment as well as fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the maximum limit for imprisonment in default of paying a fine when the offense allows both prison and fine?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Prison time for non-payment of a fine cannot exceed twenty-five percent of the maximum prison term allowed for that specific offense.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: For Zahid's case, the court must limit his additional jail time to one-fourth of the maximum term prescribed for his crime.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 65, the maximum extra jail time for not paying the fine cannot exceed one-fourth of the maximum prison term allowed for that specific offense.

Question: Scenario-based: An attacker threatens Rahim with a knife but hasn't actually touched him yet. Rahim punches the attacker. Has Rahim's right to defend himself already started?

Possible Sections.Full Text: The right of private defence of the body commences as soon as a reasonable apprehension of danger to the body arises from an attempt or threat to commit the offence though the offence may not have been committed; and it continues as long as such apprehension of danger to the body continues.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the right of private defense begin before physical contact is made?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The legal right to defend oneself starts the moment there is a reasonable fear of bodily danger due to a threat or attempt.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the knife threat created a reasonable fear for Rahim's safety, his right of private defense had already legally commenced.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 102, Rahim's right of self-defense began as soon as he had a reasonable fear of danger from the knife threat, even before being hit.

Question: Scenario-based: A person agrees to pay for a life insurance policy, but the person who was supposed to be insured dies the very next day before the first payment. Must the buyer still pay?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Notwithstanding anything contained in section 56 of the Contract Act, a contract is not wholly impossible of performance because a portion of its subject-matter, existing at its date, has ceased to exist at the time of the performance.


    Illustrations:


    (a) A contracts to sell a house to B for a lakh of taka. The day after the contract is made the house is destroyed by a cyclone. B may be compelled to perform his part of the contract by paying the purchase-money.


    (b) In consideration of a sum of money payable by B, A contracts to grant an annuity to B for B's life. The day after the contract has been made, B is thrown from his horse and killed. B's representative may be compelled to pay the purchase-money.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a contract still enforceable if the subject matter (a person's life) ceases to exist shortly after the deal is made?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A contract is not impossible to perform just because part of its subject matter ceased to exist after the contract was signed.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Even though the person died, the obligation to pay under the contract remains enforceable under Section 13.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Specific Relief Act, 1877, section 13, Illustration (b), the buyer's representative can still be forced to pay the money even though the person whose life was involved has died.

Question: Scenario-based: I want to prove that the government officially changed the name of a district. Can I use a copy of the formal Government notification to prove this fact in court?

Possible Sections.Full Text: When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it, made in a recital contained in any Act of Parliament of the United Kingdom, or in any Act of Parliament or in a Government notification, is a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a formal government notification relevant to proving a fact of a public nature?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Any statement about a public fact found within a Government notification or an Act of Parliament is relevant evidence.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The renaming of a district is a 'fact of a public nature,' making the formal Government notification relevant under the criteria of Section 37.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 37, the Government notification is a relevant fact because it contains a statement about a fact of a public nature.

Question: A court wants to call a person to testify. What three specific formal details must be present on the summons document for it to be considered legal?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 68(1)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) Every summons issued by a Court under this Code shall be in writing in duplicate, signed and sealed by the presiding officer of such Court, or by such other officer as the 78[Supreme Court] may, from time to time, by rule, direct.


    (2) Such summons shall be served by a police-officer, or subject to such rules as the Government may prescribe in this behalf, by an officer of the Court issuing it or other public servant.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 68(1)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What are the mandatory formal components required for a court-issued summons to be legally enforceable?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A summons must be drafted in writing in two copies, bear the official court seal, and carry the presiding officer's signature.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: For the document to be valid, Section 68(1) requires the presence of the officer's signature, the official seal, and the production of a duplicate copy.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 68, Subsection (1), a summons must be in writing and in duplicate, and it must be both signed and sealed by the presiding court officer.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 68(1)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: After a struggle in an office, the police found a knocked-over chair and a broken glass window. Can these signs of a fight be used in court to prove the incident happened?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Facts which are the occasions, cause, or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.


Illustrations:


(a) The question is whether A robbed B.


The facts that, shortly before the robbery, B went to a fair with money in his possession and that he showed it or mentioned the fact that he had it to third persons, are relevant.


(b) The question is whether A murdered B.


Marks on the ground, produced by a struggle at or near the place where the murder was committed, are relevant facts.


(c) The question is whether A poisoned B.


The state of B's health before the symptoms ascribed to poison, and habits of B, known to A, which afforded an opportunity for the administration of poison, are relevant facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are physical effects left after a struggle, such as broken furniture, considered relevant facts?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Facts that are the immediate effects of a fact in issue, or provide evidence of the state of things, are legally relevant.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The knocked-over chair and broken window are direct physical 'effects' of the struggle, making them relevant facts under the criteria of Section 7.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 7, Illustration (b), yes. The broken glass and chair are relevant because they are physical effects of the struggle that took place in the office.

Question: Scenario-based: A person is being held in a private house against their will. Can a family member ask the High Court Division to order that the person be brought to court immediately?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 491(1)(b)

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 491(1)(b)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the High Court Division have the authority to order the release of a person illegally detained in private custody?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The High Court can issue directions to set at liberty any person who is found to be illegally or improperly detained in any custody.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the person is being held illegally in a private house, Section 491(1)(b) allows the High Court to intervene and order their immediate release or production in court.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 491, Subsection (1), Clause (b), the High Court Division can order that a person illegally held in a house be set free immediately.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 491(1)(b)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: Someone accidentally broke an expensive piece of equipment belonging to a neighbor. This wasn't a contract issue, just an accident. How long does the neighbor have to sue for the repair costs?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 36
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for any malfeasance, misfeasance or non-feasance independent of contract and not herein specially provided for. Two years.. When the misfeasance, misfeasance or non-feasance takes place.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 36

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a civil wrong (tort) not specifically covered elsewhere in the schedule?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A suit for compensation for wrongs independent of contract must be filed within two years of the incident occurring.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since breaking the equipment is a wrong independent of contract, Article 36 applies. The neighbor must bring their suit for compensation within two years of the date the damage took place.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 36, the neighbor has two years to sue for compensation — the time starts on the day the equipment was actually broken.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 36

Question: Scenario-based: While fixing a roof, Kamal accidentally knocks a heavy tile off the edge without looking down. The tile hits a passerby on the sidewalk below, who dies instantly. Is Kamal guilty of murder?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does causing an accidental death through a reckless failure to take proper care constitute murder?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A person is liable for causing death if the fatality results from a careless or reckless act that lacks the specific intent required for homicide.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Kamal's failure to check the sidewalk before dropping materials is a negligent act causing death, which triggers Section 304A rather than murder provisions.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 304A, Kamal is not guilty of murder, but he is liable for causing death by negligence because his reckless act killed a passerby.

Question: Scenario-based: During a 'revision' hearing at the High Court, can the judge make a new order that hurts the accused person's case without letting the accused person speak first?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 439(2)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) In the case of any proceeding the record of which has been called for by itself or which has been reported for orders, or which otherwise comes to its knowledge, the High Court Division may, in its discretion, exercise any of the powers conferred on a Court of Appeal by sections 423, 426, 427 and 428 or on a Court by section 338, and may enhance the sentence; and, when the Judges composing the Court of Revision are equally divided in opinion, the case shall be disposed of in manner provided by section 429.


    (2) No order under this section shall be made to the prejudice of the accused unless he has had an opportunity of being heard either personally or by pleader in his own defence.


    (3) Where the sentence dealt with under this section has been passed by a Magistrate 457[* * *], the Court shall not inflict a greater punishment for the offence which, in the opinion of such Court, the accused has committed than might have been inflicted for such offence by 458[a Metropolitan Magistrate or] a Magistrate of the first class.


    459[(4) Nothing in this section shall be deemed to authorize the High Court Division to convert a finding of acquittal into one of conviction, or to entertain any proceedings in revision with respect to an order made by the Sessions Judge under section 439A].


    (5) Where under this Code an appeal lies and no appeal is brought, no proceedings by way of revision shall be entertained at the instance of the party who could have appealed.


    (6) Notwithstanding anything contained in this section, any convicted person to whom an opportunity has been given under sub-section (2) of showing cause why his sentence should not be enhanced shall, in showing cause, be entitled also to show cause against his conviction.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 439(2)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a revision court permitted to deliver a harmful order against an accused person without a hearing?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The High Court cannot pass any order that negatively affects the accused in a revision case unless that person has been given a chance to defend themselves.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Under Section 439, Subsection (2), any prejudicial order made without allowing the accused or their lawyer to be heard would be a direct violation of mandatory procedural law.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 439, Subsection (2), the judge cannot make an order against the accused unless the accused person or their lawyer has been given a chance to be heard.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 439(2)

Question: Scenario-based: My business dispute is being heard by a specific officer legally allowed to take evidence, though he is not a judge. Does the law view this person as a 'Court'?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:-


"Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence.


"Fact" means and includes:-


(1) anything, state of things, or relation of things capable of being perceived by the senses;


(2) any mental condition of which any person is conscious.


Illustrations:


(a) That there are certain objects arranged in a certain order in a certain place, is a fact.


(b) That a man heard or saw something, is a fact.


(c) That a man said certain words, is a fact.


(d) That a man holds a certain opinion, has a certain intention, acts in good faith or fraudulently, or uses a particular word in a particular sense, or is or was at a specified time conscious of a particular sensation, is a fact.


(e) That a man has a certain reputation, is a fact.


One fact is said to be relevant to another when the one is connected with the other in any of the ways referred to in the provisions of this Act relating to the relevancy of facts.


The expression "facts in issue" means and includes any fact from which, either by itself or in connection with other facts, the existence, non-existence, nature or extent of any right, liability, or disability, asserted or denied in any suit or proceeding, necessarily follows.


Explanation: Whenever, under the provisions of the law for the time being in force relating to Civil Procedure, any Court records an issue of fact, the fact to be asserted or denied in the answer to such issue is a fact in issue.


Illustrations:


A is accused of the murder of B.


At his trial the following facts may be in issue:-


that A caused B's death;


that A intended to cause B's death;


that A had received grave and sudden provocation from B;


that A, at the time of doing the act which caused B's death, was, by reason of unsoundness of mind, incapable of knowing its nature.


"Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter and includes any digital record.


Illustrations:


A writing is a document.


Words printed, lithographed or photographed are documents.


A map or plan is a document.


An inscription on a metal plate or stone is a document.


A caricature is a document.


"Evidence" means and includes:-


(1) all statements which the Court permits or requires to be made before it by witnesses, in relation to matters of fact under inquiry: such statements are called oral evidence;


(2) all documents produced for the inspection of the Court; such documents are called documentary evidence.


A fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist, or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it exists.


A fact is said to be disproved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes that it does not exist, or considers its non-existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it does not exist.


A fact is said not to be proved when it is neither proved nor disproved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person legally authorized to record evidence, who is not a judge, defined as a 'Court'?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The statutory definition of 'Court' encompasses all judges and any individual legally empowered to receive evidence, excluding only arbitrators.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: If the officer is legally authorized to take evidence, they fall within the broad definition of Section 3, regardless of their official title.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, any person legally authorized to take evidence is considered a 'Court.' This definition includes all judges and magistrates but excludes arbitrators.

Question: Scenario-based: Arif is suing his neighbor over a boundary wall. At the first hearing, Arif is present, but the neighbor is missing even though the court confirms he received the summons. Can the judge continue the case without him?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 6(1)(a)
Possible Sections.Full Text: Where the plaintiff appears and the defendant does not appear when the suit is called on for hearing, then— (a) if it is proved that the summons was duly served, the Court may proceed ex parte.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 6(1)(a)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: May a court conduct a trial and issue a judgment when a served defendant fails to appear at the hearing?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A judge is authorized to hear a case ex parte if the plaintiff is present and service of summons is verified.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the neighbor was properly served but failed to attend the hearing, Rule 6(1)(a) allows the court to proceed with the trial immediately.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 6, Sub-rule (1)(a), the court may proceed to hear the case ex parte, provided it is proven that the neighbor was properly served with the summons.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order IX, Rule 6(1)(a)

Question: Scenario-based: After a fight in a park, the police found scuff marks and deep footprints in the mud where the struggle happened. Can these marks be used in court to show that a fight actually took place?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Facts which are the occasions, cause, or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.


Illustrations:


(a) The question is whether A robbed B.


The facts that, shortly before the robbery, B went to a fair with money in his possession and that he showed it or mentioned the fact that he had it to third persons, are relevant.


(b) The question is whether A murdered B.


Marks on the ground, produced by a struggle at or near the place where the murder was committed, are relevant facts.


(c) The question is whether A poisoned B.


The state of B's health before the symptoms ascribed to poison, and habits of B, known to A, which afforded an opportunity for the administration of poison, are relevant facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are physical effects left at a crime scene, such as struggle marks, considered relevant evidence?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Facts that are the immediate effects of a fact in issue, or provide evidence of the state of things, are legally relevant.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The scuff marks and footprints are direct physical 'effects' of the struggle, which makes them relevant facts under the criteria of Section 7, Illustration (b).
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 7, Illustration (b), the marks found in the mud are relevant because they are physical effects of the struggle that took place.

Question: Scenario-based: I have a blood sample from a crime scene that I want to use to link a suspect to the event. What is the formal name for this type of evidence?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:-

(2) any mental condition of which any person is conscious.

(b) That a man heard or saw something, is a fact.


(c) That a man said certain words, is a fact.


(d) That a man holds a certain opinion, has a certain intention, acts in good faith or fraudulently, or uses a particular word in a particular sense, or is or was at a specified time conscious of a particular sensation, is a fact.

that A, at the time of doing the act which caused B's death, was, by reason of unsoundness of mind, incapable of knowing its nature.

"Evidence" means and includes:-

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the legal classification for biological materials like blood used to establish an offence?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The Act classifies materials such as blood, DNA, and hair used to link an offender to a crime as 'physical or forensic evidence.'
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The blood sample is a biological material used for linkage, triggering the forensic evidence definition found in Section 3, Clause (3).
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, Clause (3), a blood sample used to link a suspect to a crime is formally classified as 'physical or forensic evidence.'

Question: Scenario-based: I am accused of giving a cashier a fake Tk. 500 note. I say it was an accident. Can the prosecutor show that I tried to use fake notes at two other stores that same morning?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.


Illustrations:


(a) A is accused of burning down his house in order to obtain money for which it is insured.


The facts that A lived in several houses successively each of which he insured, in each of which a fire occurred, and after each of which fires A received payment from a different insurance office, are relevant, as tending to show that the fires were not accidental.


(b) A is employed to receive money from the debtors of B. It is A's duty to make entries in a book showing the amounts received by him. He makes an entry showing that on a particular occasion he received less than he really did receive.


The question is, whether this false entry was accidental or intentional.


The facts that other entries made by A in the same book are false, and that the false entry is in each case in favour of A, are relevant.


(c) A is accused of fraudulently delivering to B a counterfeit Taka.


The question is, whether the delivery of the Taka was accidental.


The facts that, soon before or soon after the delivery to B, A delivered counterfeit Taka to C, D and E are relevant, as showing that the delivery to B was not accidental.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are prior similar acts relevant to determining if a particular act was accidental or intentional?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A series of similar occurrences involving the same person is relevant to prove whether an act was done intentionally or by mistake.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The other fake note attempts form a 'series of similar occurrences,' which Section 15, Illustration (c) makes relevant to showing the man's act was intentional.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 15, Illustration (c), yes. The fact that you used fake notes elsewhere is relevant because it helps show your act was not an accident.

Question: Scenario-based: A man is caught doing something that is prohibited by a small local regulation but not by the main Penal Code. Does the law still consider this an 'offence'?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 4(1)(o)
Possible Sections.Full Text: "offence" means any act or omission made punishable by any law for the time being in force; it also includes any act in respect of which a complaint may be made under section 20 of the Cattle-trespass Act, 1871;

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 4(1)(o)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the legal definition of an 'offence' extend to acts punishable by laws outside the primary Penal Code?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The term covers any act or omission rendered punishable by any active law, including specific complaints under the Cattle-trespass Act.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the local regulation prescribes punishment, the man's act qualifies as an offence under Section 4(1)(o), as the definition is not restricted to the Penal Code.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 4, Subsection (1), Clause (o), the law considers any act or omission an offence if it is punishable by any law currently in force.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 4(1)(o)
