Question: Scenario-based: Someone is accused of the crime of rape. Is this a case that a standard local magistrate can handle, or must it go to a special higher court?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 376
Possible Sections.Full Text: Rape. Arrest: May arrest without warrant. Warrant or Summons: Warrant. Compoundable: Not compoundable. Punishment: Death, or transportation for life, and fine. Bailable: Not bailable. Court_Triable: Court of Session.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 376

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which court possesses the jurisdiction to try a case involving the offence of rape?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: According to the Second Schedule of the CrPC, the trial for the offence of rape is exclusively triable by the Court of Session.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As per the eighth column for Section 376 in Schedule II, only the Court of Session has the legal authority to try this serious offence, not a standard magistrate.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 376, a rape case must be tried by the Court of Session and cannot be handled by a standard magistrate.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 376
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A man bought a laptop that he knew was stolen. If he is found guilty, what is the longest amount of time he could spend in prison?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 411
Possible Sections.Full Text: [Omitted by the Schedule of the Adaptation of Central Acts and Ordinance, 1949.]

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 411

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the maximum statutory imprisonment for the offence of receiving stolen property?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The punishment for dishonestly receiving stolen property is established as up to three years of imprisonment, a fine, or both.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Based on the 'Punishment' column for Section 411 IPC in Schedule II, the court has the authority to sentence the man to a maximum of three years.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 411, the maximum punishment for receiving stolen property is three years in prison, or a fine, or both.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 411
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A captain of a large ship sees a small boat with 30 people in its path. To avoid hitting them, he must steer into another boat with only 2 people. He chooses the smaller risk. Is he guilty of a crime?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Nothing is an offence merely by reason of its being done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause harm, if it be done without any criminal intention to cause harm, and in good faith for the purpose of preventing or avoiding other harm to person or property.


Explanation: It is a question of fact in such a case whether the harm to be prevented or avoided was of such a nature and so imminent as to justify or excuse the risk of doing the act with the knowledge that it was likely to cause harm.


Illustrations:
(a) A, the captain of a steam vessel, suddenly and without any fault or negligence on his part, finds himself in such a position that, before he can stop his vessel, he must inevitably run down a boat B, with twenty or thirty passengers on board, unless he changes the course of his vessel, and that, by changing his course, he must incur risk of running down a boat C with only two passengers on board, which he may possibly clear. Here, if A alters his course without any intention to run down the boat C and in good faith for the purpose of avoiding the danger to the passengers in the boat B, he is not guilty of an offence, though he may run down the boat C by doing an act which he knew was likely to cause that effect, if it be found as a matter of fact that the danger which he intended to avoid was such as to excuse him in incurring the risk of running down C.


(b) A, is in a great fire, pulls down houses in order to prevent the conflagration from spreading. He does this with intention in good faith of saving human life or property. Here if it be found that the harm to be prevented was of such a nature and so imminent as to excuse A's act, A is not guilty of the offence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a person liable for choosing a lesser harm to prevent a greater disaster without criminal intent?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Acts likely to cause harm are not offenses if done in good faith, without criminal intent, to prevent a more severe injury.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The captain's choice to save thirty lives by risking two constitutes a valid defense of necessity under Section 81's first illustration.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 81, Illustration (a), the captain is not guilty because he acted in good faith to prevent a much greater harm without any criminal intent.

Question: Scenario-based: A judge has already signed his final decision in a case. Ten minutes later, he realizes he wrote the wrong date for the crime by mistake. Is he allowed to go back and fix just that one number?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 369
Possible Sections.Full Text: Save as otherwise provided by this Code or by any other law for the time being in force, no Court when it has signed its judgment, shall alter or review the same, except to correct a clerical error.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 369

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can a signed judgment be modified to correct a simple typographical or dating mistake?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A court is strictly prohibited from altering or reviewing a signed judgment, with the single exception of fixing clerical errors.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because writing the wrong date is a simple 'clerical error,' the judge is legally permitted under Section 369 to make that specific correction despite having already signed the document.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 369, the judge is allowed to fix the mistake because it is considered a clerical error, even after the judgment is signed.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 369
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: Kamal's last day to file his appeal is Friday, but the court is closed for a public holiday that day. When is the earliest or latest he can submit his papers?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Where the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, appeal or application expires on a day when the Court is closed, the suit, appeal or application may be instituted, preferred or made on the day that the Court re-opens.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a deadline extend automatically if it falls on a day the court is officially closed?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: When a limitation period ends on a holiday or weekend, the filing period extends to the first subsequent day the court resumes business.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the court is closed on Kamal's final day, Section 4 allows him to file his appeal on the specific date the court re-opens. This protects the right of filing from administrative closures.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 4, Kamal may file his appeal on the day the court re-opens — the law extends his deadline automatically because the original final day fell during a court closure.

Question: Scenario-based: Zahid is serving 4 months in jail for failing to pay a 400 Taka fine. After serving 2 months, his family pays 200 Taka. Does he have to serve the rest of the time?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
If, before the expiration of the term of imprisonment fixed in default of payment, such a proportion of the fine be paid or levied that the term of imprisonment suffered in default of payment is not less than proportional to the part of the fine still unpaid, the imprisonment shall terminate.


Illustration:


A is sentenced to a fine of one hundred taka and four months imprisonment in default of payment. Here, if seventy-five taka of the fine be paid or levied before the expiration of one month of the imprisonment, A will be discharged as soon as the first month has expired. If seventy-five taka be paid or levied at the time of the expiration of the first month, or at any later time while A continues in imprisonment, A will be immediately discharged. If fifty taka of the fine be paid or levied before the expiration of the two months of the imprisonments, A will be discharged as soon as the two months are completed. If fifty taka be paid or levied at the time the expiration of those two months, or at any later time while A continues in imprisonment, A will be immediately discharged.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does partial payment of a fine reduce the remaining jail time proportionally?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: If a portion of the fine is paid, the imprisonment terminates when the time served is proportional to the remaining unpaid amount.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Zahid served half his time and paid half his fine; therefore, his remaining jail term should be terminated under the proportionality rule.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 69, Zahid's imprisonment should terminate because he has paid a portion of the fine proportional to the remaining time.

Question: Scenario-based: A witness says they heard a specific person shouting certain words during a heated argument. Is the fact that these words were shouted considered a 'fact' in a legal sense?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:-


"Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence.


"Fact" means and includes:-


(1) anything, state of things, or relation of things capable of being perceived by the senses;


(2) any mental condition of which any person is conscious.


Illustrations:


(a) That there are certain objects arranged in a certain order in a certain place, is a fact.


(b) That a man heard or saw something, is a fact.


(c) That a man said certain words, is a fact.


(d) That a man holds a certain opinion, has a certain intention, acts in good faith or fraudulently, or uses a particular word in a particular sense, or is or was at a specified time conscious of a particular sensation, is a fact.


(e) That a man has a certain reputation, is a fact.


One fact is said to be relevant to another when the one is connected with the other in any of the ways referred to in the provisions of this Act relating to the relevancy of facts.


The expression "facts in issue" means and includes any fact from which, either by itself or in connection with other facts, the existence, non-existence, nature or extent of any right, liability, or disability, asserted or denied in any suit or proceeding, necessarily follows.


Explanation: Whenever, under the provisions of the law for the time being in force relating to Civil Procedure, any Court records an issue of fact, the fact to be asserted or denied in the answer to such issue is a fact in issue.


Illustrations:


A is accused of the murder of B.


At his trial the following facts may be in issue:-


that A caused B's death;


that A intended to cause B's death;


that A had received grave and sudden provocation from B;


that A, at the time of doing the act which caused B's death, was, by reason of unsoundness of mind, incapable of knowing its nature.


"Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter and includes any digital record.


Illustrations:


A writing is a document.


Words printed, lithographed or photographed are documents.


A map or plan is a document.


An inscription on a metal plate or stone is a document.


A caricature is a document.


"Evidence" means and includes:-


(1) all statements which the Court permits or requires to be made before it by witnesses, in relation to matters of fact under inquiry: such statements are called oral evidence;


(2) all documents produced for the inspection of the Court; such documents are called documentary evidence.


A fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist, or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it exists.


A fact is said to be disproved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes that it does not exist, or considers its non-existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it does not exist.


A fact is said not to be proved when it is neither proved nor disproved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is the vocalization of specific words by a person legally classified as a 'fact'?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The definition of a fact includes anything capable of being perceived by the senses, such as hearing someone speak.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Hearing someone shout words is a sense perception; Section 3, Illustration (c) explicitly confirms that a person saying specific words constitutes a fact.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, Illustration (c), the fact that a man said certain words is legally considered a 'fact' that can be used in court.

Question: Scenario-based: The judge wrote the final order but accidentally typed the wrong date by mistake. Can the judge fix this simple typing error without starting a whole new trial or appeal?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 152
Possible Sections.Full Text: Clerical or arithmetical mistakes in judgments, decrees or orders or errors arising therein from any accidental slip or omission may at any time be corrected by the Court either of its own motion or on the application of any of the parties.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 152

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a court permitted to correct obvious clerical mistakes in a final judgment without formal litigation?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Judges may fix typing or calculation errors in their own orders at any time, either on their own or when a party asks.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the wrong date is a clerical 'accidental slip,' Section 152 allows the judge to simply update the document with the correct information immediately.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 152, the judge can fix the typing error in the date at any time without needing a whole new trial.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 152
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: Amin signed a formal contract that was officially 'registered' to rent a building. The other person broke the contract today. Is the deadline to sue different because the contract was registered?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 116
Possible Sections.Full Text: For compensation for the breach of a contract in writing registered. Six years.. When the period of limitation would begin to run against a suit brought on a similar contract not registered.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 116

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a registered written contract have a different limitation period than an unregistered one?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Breach of a registered written contract allows for a six-year limitation period, whereas unregistered contracts typically allow only three years.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because Amin's contract was registered, Article 116 applies. He has a six-year window starting from today's breach to file a suit for compensation, doubling the standard three-year period.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 116, Amin has six years to sue because his contract was officially registered — this is twice as long as the usual three-year limit.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 116

Question: Scenario-based: A police officer sees a man physically attacking someone in the middle of a park. Does the officer need to go to a judge to get a warrant before he can stop the fight and arrest the attacker?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 54(1)

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 54(1)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can an officer perform an immediate arrest without a warrant if they witness a crime occurring in their presence?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Warrantless arrest is legally permitted for any person who commits a cognizable offense while in the direct view of a police officer.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the assault is a cognizable offense happening right in front of the officer, Section 54(1) authorizes an immediate arrest without any prior judicial order.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 54, Subsection (1), Firstly, the police officer can arrest the attacker without a warrant because the crime was committed in his presence.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 54(1)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A Muslim woman has a right to 'exigible dower' in her marriage contract. She asked her husband for the payment today, but he refused. How long does she have to sue him for it?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 103
Possible Sections.Full Text: By a Muslim for exigible dower (*mu’ajjal*). Three years.. When the dower is demanded and refused or (where, during the continuance of the marriage no such demand has been made) when the marriage is dissolved by death or divorce.

Relevant Section: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 103

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the limitation period for a suit for exigible dower and what triggers the start of the period?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A suit for exigible dower must be filed within three years of a demand and refusal, or upon the dissolution of the marriage.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the woman made a demand today and it was refused, Article 103 triggers a three-year limitation period starting from today for her to file a lawsuit for the dower.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 103, the woman has three years to sue for her dower — the time starts now because her demand was refused today.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Limitation Act, 1908, Schedule I, Article 103

Question: Scenario-based: Someone is accused of committing murder. Which specific level of the court has the authority to hold the final trial for this crime?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 302
Possible Sections.Full Text: Murder. Arrest: May arrest without warrant. Warrant or Summons: Warrant. Compoundable: Not compoundable. Punishment: Death, or transportation for life, and fine. Bailable: Not bailable. Court_Triable: Court of Session.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 302

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Which judicial body holds the specific subject matter jurisdiction to try a murder case?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The trial of an offense classified as murder under Section 302 of the Penal Code is assigned exclusively to the Court of Session.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: According to the eighth column of Schedule II for Section 302, only the Court of Session possesses the legal authority to adjudicate this extremely serious crime.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 302, the case must be tried by the Court of Session, as it is the only court authorized to hear murder cases.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 302
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim and Karim are arguing over whether a signature on a deed is forged. Rahim says it is real, and Karim says it is fake. Can Rahim prove that Karim once told a friend the signature was genuine?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they cannot be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–


(1) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, when it is of such a nature that, if the person making it were dead, it would be relevant as between third persons under section 32.


(2) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, when it consists of a statement of the existence of any state of mind or body, relevant or in issue, made at or about the time when such state of mind or body existed, and is accompanied by conduct rendering its falsehood improbable.


(3) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, if it is relevant otherwise than as an admission.


Illustrations:


(a) The question between A and B is, whether a certain deed is or is not forged. A affirms that it is genuine, B that it is forged.


A may prove a statement by B that the deed is genuine, and B may prove a statement by A that the deed is forged; but A cannot prove a statement by himself that the deed is genuine, nor can B prove a statement by himself that the deed is forged.


(b) A, the captain of a ship, is tried for casting her away.


Evidence is given to show that the ship was taken out of her proper course.


A produces a book kept by him in the ordinary course of his business showing observations alleged to have been taken by him from day to day, and indicating that the ship was not taken out of her proper course. A may prove these statements, because they would be admissible between third parties, if he were dead, under section 32, clause (2).


(c) A is accused of a crime committed by him at Chittagong.


He produces a letter written by himself and dated at Dhaka on that day and bearing the Dhaka post-mark of that day.


The statement in the date of the letter is admissible, because, if A were dead it would be admissible under section 32, clause (2).


(d) A is accused of receiving stolen goods knowing them to be stolen.


He offers to prove that he refused to sell them below their value.


A may prove these statements, though they are admissions, because they are explanatory of conduct influenced by facts in issue.


(e) A is accused of fraudulently having in his possession counterfeit coin which he knew to be counterfeit.


He offers to prove that he asked a skilful person to examine the coin as he doubted whether it was counterfeit or not, and that that person did examine it and told him it was genuine.


A may prove these facts for the reasons stated in the last preceding illustration.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an admission by one party regarding a fact in issue admissible when proved by the opposing party?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Admissions are relevant and can be proved against the person who made them by the opposing side.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Karim’s prior statement that the signature was genuine contradicts his current claim of forgery; Rahim may prove this admission against Karim under Section 21.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 21, Illustration (a), Rahim can prove your previous statement that the deed was genuine because admissions are relevant when proved against the person who made them.

Question: Scenario-based: Karim is accused of taking part in a violent protest where a building was burned and a car was smashed. Karim says he wasn't at the building fire. Can the fire be mentioned in his trial for the car smashing?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Facts which, though not in issue, are so connected with a fact in issue as to form part of the same transaction, are relevant, whether they occurred at the same time and place or at different times and places.


Illustrations:


(a) A is accused of the murder of B by beating him. Whatever was said or done by A or B or the bystanders at the beating, or so shortly before or after it as to form part of the transaction, is a relevant fact.


(b) A is accused of waging war against Bangladesh by taking part in an armed insurrection in which property is destroyed, troops are attacked, and jails are broken open. The occurrence of these facts is relevant, as forming part of the general transaction, though A may not have been present at all of them.


(c) A sues B for a libel contained in a letter forming part of a correspondence. Letters between the parties relating to the subject out of which the libel arose, and forming part of the correspondence in which it is contained, are relevant facts, though they do not contain the libel itself.


(d) The question is whether certain goods ordered from B were delivered to A. The goods were delivered to several intermediate persons successively. Each delivery is a relevant fact.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are separate acts of violence relevant if they form part of the same general transaction or insurrection?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Facts that are connected as part of a single transaction are relevant even if they happened at different times or the accused was not present at every act.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The fire and car smashing are part of the same protest 'transaction'; thus, Section 6 makes the fire relevant to Karim’s case even if he wasn't at that specific spot.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 6, Illustration (b), the fire can be mentioned because it is part of the same general transaction as the car smashing, even if Karim wasn't present at the fire.

Question: Scenario-based: Javed joins a group of 10 people gathered for an illegal purpose. Unlike the others, Javed is carrying a loaded gun. Does he face a longer sentence than the unarmed members of the group?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever, being armed with any deadly weapon, or with anything which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, is a member of an unlawful assembly, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is there an enhanced penalty for being armed while participating in an unlawful assembly?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Members of an unlawful assembly who carry deadly weapons face a maximum sentence of two years, which is higher than the standard six-month term.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Javed's possession of a loaded gun while in the assembly triggers Section 144, allowing for a sentence of up to two years.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 144, Javed faces a harsher sentence of up to two years in prison because he was armed with a deadly weapon.

Question: Scenario-based: A person won a money case in a superior court in the United Kingdom. Can they bring an official copy of that judgment to a Bangladesh court and have it enforced as if it was made here?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 44A(1)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) Where a certified copy of a decree of any of the superior Courts of 32[* * *] any reciprocating territory has been filed in a District Court, the decree may be executed in Bangladesh as if it had been passed by the District Court.


(2) Together with the certified copy of the decree shall be filed a certificate from such superior Court stating the extent, if any, to which the decree has been satisfied or adjusted and such certificate shall, for the purposes of proceedings under this section, be conclusive proof of the extent of such satisfaction or adjustment.


(3) The provisions of section 47 shall as from the filing of the certified copy of the decree apply to the proceedings of a District Court executing a decree under this section, and the District Court shall refuse execution of any such decree, if it is shown to the satisfaction of the Court that the decree falls within any of the exceptions specified in clauses (a) to (f) of section 13.]


33[* * *]


Explanation 2.- "Reciprocating territory" means 34[any] country or territory as the Government may, from time to time, by notification in the official Gazette, declare to be reciprocating territory for the purposes of this section; and "Superior Courts", with reference to any such territory, means such Courts as may be specified in the said notification.


Explanation 3.- "Decree", with reference to a superior Court, means any decree or judgment of such Court under which a sum of money is payable, not being a sum payable in respect of taxes or other charges of a like nature or in respect of a fine or other penalty, and


35[* * *]


(b) in no case includes an arbitration award, even if such award is enforceable as a decree or judgment.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 44A(1)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a judgment from a foreign superior court directly enforceable in Bangladesh without a new trial?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Decrees from reciprocating territories like the UK can be enforced by a local District Court as its own judgment.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Filing the UK decree in a District Court invokes Section 44A(1), allowing for local execution under the reciprocating status of the territory.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 44A, Subsection (1), the judgment from the United Kingdom can be enforced in Bangladesh just like a decision made by a local District Court.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 44A(1)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: Someone stole a bag of groceries from a store. Does the police officer need to wait for a judge to sign a warrant before catching the thief?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 379
Possible Sections.Full Text: In cases submitted by the Court of Session to the High Court Division for the confirmation of a sentence of death, the proper officer of the High Court Division shall, without delay, after the order of confirmation or other order has been made by the High Court Division, send a copy of the order, under the seal of the High Court Division and attested with his official signature, to the Court of Session.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 379

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a standard theft charge a cognizable offence that permits an arrest without a judicial warrant?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The law classifies theft as an offence for which the police possess the authority to arrest individuals without a prior warrant.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: According to the 'Arrest' column for Section 379 IPC in Schedule II, the officer may arrest the bag thief immediately because theft is a cognizable crime.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 379, the police are authorized to arrest the person for theft without waiting for a warrant from a judge.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 379
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: Someone reports a minor crime to a police officer that is not considered a 'serious' (cognizable) offence. Can the officer immediately start investigating the case on his own?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 155(2)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) When information is given to an officer in charge of a police station of the commission within the limits of such station of a non-cognizable offence, he shall enter in a book to be kept as aforesaid the substance of such information and refer the informant to the Magistrate.


    (2) No police officer shall investigate a non-cognizable case without the order of a Magistrate of the first or second class having power to try such case or send the same for trial.


    (3) Any police officer receiving such order may exercise the same powers in respect of the investigation (except the power to arrest without warrant) as an officer in charge of a police station may exercise in a cognizable case.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 155(2)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: May a police officer initiate an investigation into a non-cognizable case without judicial authorization?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The statute explicitly prohibits police officers from investigating non-cognizable cases unless they receive a formal order from a competent magistrate.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the reported crime is non-cognizable, Section 155(2) bars the officer from starting an investigation until he secures an order from a first or second-class magistrate.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 155, Subsection (2), a police officer is strictly forbidden from investigating a non-cognizable case without a direct order from a magistrate.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 155(2)
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A unique problem happened in a case that is not covered by any specific rule in the law books. Can the judge make a special order to ensure fairness, or is their power limited to the written rules?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151
Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing in this Code shall be deemed to limit or otherwise affect the inherent power of the Court to make such orders as may be necessary for the ends of justice or to prevent abuse of the process of the Court.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a court possess unspecified powers to ensure justice when existing rules are silent or insufficient?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Courts maintain inherent powers to issue any orders required to achieve justice or stop individuals from abusing the legal system.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: When faced with an unregulated procedural gap, a judge can use Section 151 to craft a fair solution to ensure that justice is served to the parties.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 151, the judge can make any necessary order to ensure the case is handled fairly and to prevent anyone from cheating the system.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Civil Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A woman consents to a procedure that causes her a miscarriage, purely for personal reasons and not to save her life. Does her consent make the act legal under this Code?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
The exceptions in sections 87, 88 and 89 do not extend to acts which are offences independently of any harm which they may cause, or be intended to cause, or be known to be likely to cause, to the person giving the consent, or on whose behalf the consent is given.


Illustration:
Causing miscarriage (unless caused in good faith for the purpose of saving the life of a woman) is an offence independently of any harm which it may cause or be intended to cause to the woman. Therefore, it is not an offence 'by reason of such harm'; and the consent of the woman or of her guardian to the causing of such miscarriage does not justify the act.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does consent provide a defense for acts that are categorized as offenses independently of the harm caused to the consenting party?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Legal exceptions for consent do not apply to acts that are specifically defined as illegal regardless of the victim's agreement.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since causing a miscarriage is a separate offense regardless of harm, the woman's consent does not fall under the general exceptions for consensual acts.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 91, the woman's consent does not make the act legal because the law does not allow consent for acts that are independent offenses.

Question: Scenario-based: I am on trial for a crime in Dhaka. Can I prove a letter I posted from Chittagong on that same day as evidence to show I was not in Dhaka?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Admissions are relevant and may be proved as against the person who makes them, or his representative in interest; but they cannot be proved by or on behalf of the person who makes them or by his representative in interest, except in the following cases:–


(1) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, when it is of such a nature that, if the person making it were dead, it would be relevant as between third persons under section 32.


(2) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, when it consists of a statement of the existence of any state of mind or body, relevant or in issue, made at or about the time when such state of mind or body existed, and is accompanied by conduct rendering its falsehood improbable.


(3) An admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it, if it is relevant otherwise than as an admission.


Illustrations:


(a) The question between A and B is, whether a certain deed is or is not forged. A affirms that it is genuine, B that it is forged.


A may prove a statement by B that the deed is genuine, and B may prove a statement by A that the deed is forged; but A cannot prove a statement by himself that the deed is genuine, nor can B prove a statement by himself that the deed is forged.


(b) A, the captain of a ship, is tried for casting her away.


Evidence is given to show that the ship was taken out of her proper course.


A produces a book kept by him in the ordinary course of his business showing observations alleged to have been taken by him from day to day, and indicating that the ship was not taken out of her proper course. A may prove these statements, because they would be admissible between third parties, if he were dead, under section 32, clause (2).


(c) A is accused of a crime committed by him at Chittagong.


He produces a letter written by himself and dated at Dhaka on that day and bearing the Dhaka post-mark of that day.


The statement in the date of the letter is admissible, because, if A were dead it would be admissible under section 32, clause (2).


(d) A is accused of receiving stolen goods knowing them to be stolen.


He offers to prove that he refused to sell them below their value.


A may prove these statements, though they are admissions, because they are explanatory of conduct influenced by facts in issue.


(e) A is accused of fraudulently having in his possession counterfeit coin which he knew to be counterfeit.


He offers to prove that he asked a skilful person to examine the coin as he doubted whether it was counterfeit or not, and that that person did examine it and told him it was genuine.


A may prove these facts for the reasons stated in the last preceding illustration.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Can an accused person use their own prior statement as evidence if it meets the business record exception criteria?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Parties generally cannot prove their own admissions unless the statement would be admissible under Section 32 (like a business record) if they were dead.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: A postmarked letter is a record that would be admissible under Section 32(2) if the writer were dead, allowing its use under Section 21(1).
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 21, Illustration (c), yes. You can prove your own letter because the postmark is a type of record that would be allowed if you were deceased.

Question: Scenario-based: A person gives a speech intended to make two groups of people hate each other. Does this person have the legal right to be released on bail before their trial?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 153A
Possible Sections.Full Text: Promoting enmity between classes. Arrest: Shall not arrest without warrant. Warrant or Summons: Warrant. Compoundable: Not compoundable. Punishment: Imprisonment of either description for 2 years, or fine, or both. Bailable: Not bailable.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 153A

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is the offense of promoting enmity between different classes considered a bailable crime?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The act of promoting class enmity is specifically categorized as a non-bailable offense in the legal schedule.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Based on the 'Bailable' column for Section 153A in Schedule 2, the speaker is not entitled to bail as a matter of right and must remain in custody unless the court decides otherwise.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 153A, the person does not have a right to bail because promoting enmity between classes is a non-bailable offence.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 153A

Question: Scenario-based: A strange and rare situation has occurred in a case that is not described anywhere in the written rules of the law book. Can the judge make a special order to ensure fairness, or is he forbidden from acting?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151
Possible Sections.Full Text: Nothing in this Code shall be deemed to limit or otherwise affect the inherent power of the Court to make such orders as may be necessary for the ends of justice or to prevent abuse of the process of the Court.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a court possess generalized powers to ensure justice when specific written rules are missing or silent?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Courts maintain an 'inherent power' to issue any orders necessary to achieve justice or stop someone from misusing the legal system.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: When a procedural gap exists, the judge can use Section 151 to create a fair solution that ensures the case is decided justly.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, section 151, the judge can make any order he feels is necessary to ensure justice and fairness, even if there isn't a specific rule for that situation.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Section 151

Question: Scenario-based: A man is caught giving a fake coin to a cashier. He says he didn't know it was fake. Can the prosecutor show that he gave similar fake coins to three other shops that same morning?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.


Illustrations:


(a) A is accused of burning down his house in order to obtain money for which it is insured.


The facts that A lived in several houses successively each of which he insured, in each of which a fire occurred, and after each of which fires A received payment from a different insurance office, are relevant, as tending to show that the fires were not accidental.


(b) A is employed to receive money from the debtors of B. It is A's duty to make entries in a book showing the amounts received by him. He makes an entry showing that on a particular occasion he received less than he really did receive.


The question is, whether this false entry was accidental or intentional.


The facts that other entries made by A in the same book are false, and that the false entry is in each case in favour of A, are relevant.


(c) A is accused of fraudulently delivering to B a counterfeit Taka.


The question is, whether the delivery of the Taka was accidental.


The facts that, soon before or soon after the delivery to B, A delivered counterfeit Taka to C, D and E are relevant, as showing that the delivery to B was not accidental.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a series of similar acts relevant to determining if a particular act was accidental or intentional?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A pattern or series of similar occurrences involving the same person is relevant to prove whether an act was done intentionally or by mistake.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The other three fake coin deliveries form a 'series of similar occurrences,' which Section 15, Illustration (c) makes relevant to showing the man's act was intentional.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 15, Illustration (c), the fact that you gave fake coins to other shops is relevant to proving that your act was not an accident.

Question: Scenario-based: A village claims they have a traditional right to graze cattle in a specific field. Can they show an old agreement between their grandfathers and the previous field owner to prove this?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Where the question is as to the existence of any right of custom, the following facts are relevant:–


(a) any transaction by which the right or custom in question was created, claimed, modified, recognized, asserted or denied, or which was inconsistent with its existence;


(b) particular instances in which the right or custom was claimed, recognized or exercised, or in which its exercise was disputed, asserted or departed from.


Illustration


The question is whether A has a right to a fishery. A deed conferring the fishery on A's ancestors, a mortgage of the fishery by A's father, a subsequent grant of the fishery by A's father, irreconcilable with the mortgage, particular instances in which A's father exercised the right, or in which the exercise of the right was stopped by A's neighbours, are relevant facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a historical agreement relevant to proving the existence of a specific right or custom?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: When the existence of a right is questioned, any transaction that created or recognized that right is considered a relevant fact.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The old agreement is a transaction where the grazing right was recognized; therefore, Section 13(a) makes that document relevant evidence in the current dispute.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 13, Clause (a), yes. The old agreement is a relevant fact because it is a transaction by which your traditional right was recognized.

Question: Scenario-based: I have a printed caricature of a local official that I want to use to prove public opinion in a defamation case. Is this drawing legally considered a 'document'?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:-


"Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence.


"Fact" means and includes:-


(1) anything, state of things, or relation of things capable of being perceived by the senses;


(2) any mental condition of which any person is conscious.


Illustrations:


(a) That there are certain objects arranged in a certain order in a certain place, is a fact.


(b) That a man heard or saw something, is a fact.


(c) That a man said certain words, is a fact.


(d) That a man holds a certain opinion, has a certain intention, acts in good faith or fraudulently, or uses a particular word in a particular sense, or is or was at a specified time conscious of a particular sensation, is a fact.


(e) That a man has a certain reputation, is a fact.


One fact is said to be relevant to another when the one is connected with the other in any of the ways referred to in the provisions of this Act relating to the relevancy of facts.


The expression "facts in issue" means and includes any fact from which, either by itself or in connection with other facts, the existence, non-existence, nature or extent of any right, liability, or disability, asserted or denied in any suit or proceeding, necessarily follows.


Explanation: Whenever, under the provisions of the law for the time being in force relating to Civil Procedure, any Court records an issue of fact, the fact to be asserted or denied in the answer to such issue is a fact in issue.


Illustrations:


A is accused of the murder of B.


At his trial the following facts may be in issue:-


that A caused B's death;


that A intended to cause B's death;


that A had received grave and sudden provocation from B;


that A, at the time of doing the act which caused B's death, was, by reason of unsoundness of mind, incapable of knowing its nature.


"Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter and includes any digital record.


Illustrations:


A writing is a document.


Words printed, lithographed or photographed are documents.


A map or plan is a document.


An inscription on a metal plate or stone is a document.


A caricature is a document.


"Evidence" means and includes:-


(1) all statements which the Court permits or requires to be made before it by witnesses, in relation to matters of fact under inquiry: such statements are called oral evidence;


(2) all documents produced for the inspection of the Court; such documents are called documentary evidence.


A fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist, or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it exists.


A fact is said to be disproved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes that it does not exist, or considers its non-existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it does not exist.


A fact is said not to be proved when it is neither proved nor disproved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a caricature meet the statutory definition of a document for evidentiary purposes?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: The legal definition of a document includes various items such as writings, maps, and specifically lists caricatures as valid documents.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since Section 3 explicitly includes caricatures in its definition list, the drawing qualifies as a document that may be presented as evidence.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, your caricature is legally recognized as a 'document' and can be submitted to the court as documentary evidence to support your case.

Question: Scenario-based: A police officer uses a megaphone to order an unlawful assembly to disperse. Salim hears the order but stays in the group anyway. Does Salim face a harsher sentence than simple membership?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever joins or continues in an unlawful assembly, knowing that such unlawful assembly has been commanded in the manner prescribed by law to disperse, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is there an enhanced penalty for remaining in an unlawful assembly after a lawful command to disperse?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Continuing in an unlawful assembly while knowing it has been ordered to disperse is punishable by up to two years, rather than the standard six months.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Salim heard the order but stayed, triggering the enhanced two-year penalty tier provided in Section 145.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 145, Salim faces a harsher sentence of up to two years in prison for staying in the group after it was ordered to disperse.

Question: Scenario-based: A lawsuit was filed against one person last month. Today, the plaintiff realized they needed to add another person as a defendant. For this new person, when does the law consider the lawsuit to have started?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Where, after the institution of a suit, a new plaintiff or defendant is substituted or added, the suit shall, as regards him, be deemed to have been instituted when he was so made a party.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When is a lawsuit considered to have begun for a party who is added to an existing case after it has already started?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A suit is deemed to have been instituted against an added party on the specific date they were officially joined to the proceedings.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: While the case began last month for the original defendant, Section 22(1) applies to the new person today. For limitation purposes, the suit against the new defendant starts only on the day they were added.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 22, Subsection (1), the lawsuit is considered to have started for the new person only on the day they were officially added as a party to the case.

Question: Scenario-based: In a property dispute, the plaintiff accidentally forgot to include one of the three co-owners as a party. Does this mistake mean the entire case must be thrown out by the judge?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order I, Rule 9
Possible Sections.Full Text: No suit shall be defeated by reason of the misjoinder or nonjoinder of parties, and the Court may in every suit deal with the matter in controversy so far as regards the rights and interests of the parties actually before it.

Relevant Section: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order I, Rule 9

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Will a lawsuit automatically fail if a necessary party was not included in the original filing?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A case cannot be dismissed simply because a party was incorrectly added or left out; the court must decide the issues for those currently involved.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The failure to include a co-owner is a case of nonjoinder, which according to Rule 9, is not a sufficient reason to defeat the entire suit.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order I, Rule 9, the judge cannot dismiss the whole case just because a party was left out; the court can still decide on the rights of those present.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, Order I, Rule 9

Question: Scenario-based: B owns the subsoil under A's field. B dug some coal out, which didn't hurt the surface at first. One year later, A's field suddenly sank into a hole. When does A's time to sue B start?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In the case of a suit for compensation for an act which does not give rise to a cause of action unless some specific injury actually results therefrom, the period of limitation shall be computed from the time when the injury results. 
Illustration A owns the surface of a field. B owns the subsoil. B digs coal thereout without causing any immediate apparent injury to the surface, but at last the surface subsides. The period of limitation in the case of a suit by A against B runs from the time of the subsidence.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: When does the limitation period begin for an act that only becomes actionable after specific damage occurs?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: For acts requiring specific injury for a cause of action, the limitation period commences only when that injury actually manifests.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: B's digging was not actionable until the field subsided. Under Section 24, A's limitation clock begins exactly at the moment of the subsidence, not when the digging occurred a year earlier.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 24, the time to sue starts only when the field actually sinks — the deadline is not calculated from when the digging originally happened.

Question: Scenario-based: A man encourages a soldier to punch his superior officer while the officer is performing his duties. What is the maximum time this man can be jailed for this encouragement?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever abets an assault by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman, in the Army, Navy or Air Force of Bangladesh, on any superior officer being in the execution of his office, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the penalty for abetting an assault on a military superior during the execution of their office?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Abetting an assault against a superior officer in the line of duty is punishable by a maximum of three years in prison and a fine.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the man instigated an assault on an officer performing his duties, he is subject to the three-year penalty prescribed by Section 133.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 133, the man can be punished with a maximum of three years in prison and a fine.

Question: Scenario-based: A court is attaching the property of a runaway person, including a truckload of fresh vegetables. Since the vegetables will spoil quickly, can the court sell them immediately?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 88(5)
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) The Court issuing a proclamation under section 87 may at any time order the attachment of any property, movable or immovable, or both, belonging to the proclaimed person.


    (2) Such order shall authorize the attachment of any property belonging to such person within the 100[local area] in which it is made; and it shall authorize the attachment of any property belonging to such person without such 101[local area] when endorsed by the District Magistrate 102[Chief Judicial Magistrate] 103[or Chief Metropolitan Magistrate] within whose 104[local area] such property is situate.


    (3) If the property ordered to be attached is a debt or other movable property, the attachment under this section shall be made-


    (a) by seizure; or


    (b) by the appointment of a receiver; or


    (c) by an order in writing prohibiting the delivery of such property to the proclaimed person or to any one on his behalf; or


    (d) by all or any two of such methods, as the Court thinks fit.


    (4) If the property ordered to be attached is immovable, the attachment under this section shall, in the case of land paying revenue to the Government, be made through the Collector of the district in which the land is situate, and in all other cases-


    (e) by taking possession; or


    (f) by the appointment of a receiver; or


    (g) by an order in writing prohibiting the payment of rent or delivery of property to the proclaimed person or to any one on his behalf; or


    (h) by all or any two of such methods, as the Court thinks fit.


    (5) If the property ordered to be attached consists of live-stock or is of a perishable nature, the Court may, if it thinks it expedient, order immediate sale thereof, and in such case the proceeds of the sale shall abide the order of the Court.


    (6) The powers, duties and liabilities of a receiver appointed under this section shall be the same as those of a receiver appointed under 105[Order XL of the First Schedule to the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908].


    (6A) If any claim is preferred to, or objection made to the attachment of, any property attached under this section within six months from the date of such attachment, by any person other than the proclaimed person, on the ground that the claimant or objector has an interest in such property, and that such interest is not liable to attachment under this section, the claim or objection shall be inquired into, and may be allowed or disallowed in whole or in part:


    Provided that any claim preferred or objection made within the period allowed by this sub-section may, in the event of the death of the claimant or objector, be continued by his legal representative.


    (6B) Claims or objections under sub-section (6A) may be preferred or made in the Court by which the order of attachment is issued or, if the claim or objection is in respect of property attached under an order endorsed by a District Magistrate, 106[Chief Judicial Magistrate] 107[or Chief Metropolitan Magistrate] in accordance with the provisions of sub-section (2), in the Court of such Magistrate.


    (6C) Every such claim or objection shall be inquired into by the Court in which it is preferred or made:


    Provided that, if it is preferred or made in the Court of a 108[ Chief Judicial Magistrate] 1109[or Chief Metropolitan Magistrate] such Magistrate may make it over for disposal to any Magistrate 110[***] [or to any Metropolitan Magistrate, as the case may be] subordinate to him.


    (6D) Any person whose claim or objection has been disallowed in whole or in part by an order under sub-section (6A) may, within a period of one year from the date of such order, institute a suit to establish the right which he claims in respect of the property in dispute; but subject to the result of such suit, if any, the order shall be conclusive.


    (6E) If the proclaimed person appears within the time specified in the proclamation, the Court shall make an order releasing the property from the attachment.


    (7) If the proclaimed person does not appear within the time specified in the proclamation, the property under attachment shall be at the disposal of the Government, but it shall not be sold until the expiration of six months from the date of the attachment and until any claim preferred or objection made under sub-section (6A) has been disposed of under that sub-section, unless it is subject to speedy and natural decay, or the Court considers that the sale would be for the benefit of the owner, in either of which cases the Court may cause it to be sold whenever it thinks fit.

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 88(5)

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does a court have the authority to sell attached property that is likely to decay or perish?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Property that is perishable or consists of livestock may be sold immediately by the court if it is deemed expedient to do so.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the vegetables are of a 'perishable nature,' Section 88(5) grants the court the legal power to sell them at once and hold the money for the case.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, section 88, Subsection (5), the court can order the immediate sale of attached property if it is perishable, such as fresh vegetables.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Section 88(5)

Question: Scenario-based: An accountant is accused of entering a smaller amount in the records to keep the difference. He says it was a one-time typing error. Can the boss show that he made similar 'errors' in five other books?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant.


Illustrations:


(a) A is accused of burning down his house in order to obtain money for which it is insured.


The facts that A lived in several houses successively each of which he insured, in each of which a fire occurred, and after each of which fires A received payment from a different insurance office, are relevant, as tending to show that the fires were not accidental.


(b) A is employed to receive money from the debtors of B. It is A's duty to make entries in a book showing the amounts received by him. He makes an entry showing that on a particular occasion he received less than he really did receive.


The question is, whether this false entry was accidental or intentional.


The facts that other entries made by A in the same book are false, and that the false entry is in each case in favour of A, are relevant.


(c) A is accused of fraudulently delivering to B a counterfeit Taka.


The question is, whether the delivery of the Taka was accidental.


The facts that, soon before or soon after the delivery to B, A delivered counterfeit Taka to C, D and E are relevant, as showing that the delivery to B was not accidental.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Are prior similar acts relevant to determining if a specific act was accidental or a part of an intentional pattern?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A series of similar occurrences involving the same person is relevant to prove whether an act was intentional or accidental.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The repeated 'errors' in other books form a series of similar occurrences, which Section 15 makes relevant to disprove the 'accidental' defense.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 15, Illustration (b), the other false entries are relevant because they help show that the mistake was intentional and not an accident.

Question: Scenario-based: Rahim wants to sue a man who lived in London for the last five years. Does the time that man spent outside of Bangladesh count as part of the three-year deadline for Rahim's lawsuit?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In computing the period of limitation prescribed for any suit, the time during which the defendant has been absent from Bangladesh and from the territories beyond Bangladesh under the administration of the Government shall be excluded.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is the period a defendant spends outside the country excluded from the calculation of the limitation period for a suit?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Any time the person being sued is absent from Bangladesh is deducted from the total limitation period.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since the man was in London for five years, Section 13 requires that those five years be excluded from Rahim's filing deadline. Effectively, the clock was paused while the defendant was abroad.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Limitation Act, 1908, section 13, the five years the man spent in London are excluded from the deadline — this time does not count toward the three-year limit for Rahim's lawsuit.

Question: Scenario-based: A man is convicted of a crime, and the judge wants to take away his house and land as punishment. Is this type of punishment allowed under the Penal Code?

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Does the Penal Code allow for the forfeiture of an offender's property as a form of punishment?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Statutory law lists 'forfeiture of property' as one of the six categories of legal punishments for offenders.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The judge's plan to seize the house and land is consistent with the 'Fifthly' clause of Section 53, which permits property forfeiture.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 53, Fifthly, this punishment is allowed because the law specifically includes forfeiture of property as a type of punishment for offenders.

Question: Scenario-based: A man dumps a bucket of dirty chemicals into a public village well that everyone uses for drinking. What is the maximum time he can be jailed for ruining the water?

Possible Sections.Full Text: Whoever voluntarily corrupts or fouls the water of any public spring or reservoir, so as to render it less fit for the purpose for which it is ordinarily used, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred taka or with both.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: What is the penalty for polluting a public drinking water source?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Voluntarily fouling or corrupting the water of a public spring or reservoir is punishable by up to three months in prison.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: As the well is a public reservoir and the man deliberately polluted it, he is subject to the three-month maximum sentence under Section 277.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Penal Code, 1860, section 277, the man can be punished with a maximum of three months in prison, a fine of five hundred Taka, or both.

Question: Scenario-based: In my trial, a witness claims they have a vivid memory of seeing me at a specific location. Is the witness's mental awareness of this memory considered a 'fact'?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
In this Act the following words and expressions are used in the following senses, unless a contrary intention appears from the context:-


"Court" includes all Judges and Magistrates and all persons, except arbitrators, legally authorized to take evidence.


"Fact" means and includes:-


(1) anything, state of things, or relation of things capable of being perceived by the senses;


(2) any mental condition of which any person is conscious.


Illustrations:


(a) That there are certain objects arranged in a certain order in a certain place, is a fact.


(b) That a man heard or saw something, is a fact.


(c) That a man said certain words, is a fact.


(d) That a man holds a certain opinion, has a certain intention, acts in good faith or fraudulently, or uses a particular word in a particular sense, or is or was at a specified time conscious of a particular sensation, is a fact.


(e) That a man has a certain reputation, is a fact.


One fact is said to be relevant to another when the one is connected with the other in any of the ways referred to in the provisions of this Act relating to the relevancy of facts.


The expression "facts in issue" means and includes any fact from which, either by itself or in connection with other facts, the existence, non-existence, nature or extent of any right, liability, or disability, asserted or denied in any suit or proceeding, necessarily follows.


Explanation: Whenever, under the provisions of the law for the time being in force relating to Civil Procedure, any Court records an issue of fact, the fact to be asserted or denied in the answer to such issue is a fact in issue.


Illustrations:


A is accused of the murder of B.


At his trial the following facts may be in issue:-


that A caused B's death;


that A intended to cause B's death;


that A had received grave and sudden provocation from B;


that A, at the time of doing the act which caused B's death, was, by reason of unsoundness of mind, incapable of knowing its nature.


"Document" means any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, for the purpose of recording that matter and includes any digital record.


Illustrations:


A writing is a document.


Words printed, lithographed or photographed are documents.


A map or plan is a document.


An inscription on a metal plate or stone is a document.


A caricature is a document.


"Evidence" means and includes:-


(1) all statements which the Court permits or requires to be made before it by witnesses, in relation to matters of fact under inquiry: such statements are called oral evidence;


(2) all documents produced for the inspection of the Court; such documents are called documentary evidence.


A fact is said to be proved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes it to exist, or considers its existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it exists.


A fact is said to be disproved when, after considering the matters before it, the Court either believes that it does not exist, or considers its non-existence so probable that a prudent man ought, under the circumstances of the particular case, to act upon the supposition that it does not exist.


A fact is said not to be proved when it is neither proved nor disproved.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a conscious mental condition, such as a memory, legally defined as a 'fact' under the Evidence Act?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: A fact is defined to include both physical sensations and any internal mental state of which a person is consciously aware.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Because the witness is conscious of their memory, it satisfies the definition of a mental condition fact as established in Section 3.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 3, Subsection (2), the witness's conscious memory of seeing you is legally considered a 'fact' because the law includes mental conditions in its definition.

Question: Scenario-based: A woman was poisoned. Before she showed symptoms, her husband was the only one who had access to her medicine cabinet. Can the fact that he had this exclusive access be used as evidence against him?

Possible Sections.Full Text:
Facts which are the occasions, cause, or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts, or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction, are relevant.


Illustrations:


(a) The question is whether A robbed B.


The facts that, shortly before the robbery, B went to a fair with money in his possession and that he showed it or mentioned the fact that he had it to third persons, are relevant.


(b) The question is whether A murdered B.


Marks on the ground, produced by a struggle at or near the place where the murder was committed, are relevant facts.


(c) The question is whether A poisoned B.


The state of B's health before the symptoms ascribed to poison, and habits of B, known to A, which afforded an opportunity for the administration of poison, are relevant facts.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is the exclusive opportunity to commit an act a relevant fact under the Evidence Act?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Facts that constitute the state of things or provide an opportunity for an event to occur are considered relevant evidence.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: The husband's sole access to the medicine cabinet created the specific opportunity to administer the poison, triggering relevancy under Section 7, Illustration (c).
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Evidence Act, 1872, section 7, Illustration (c), the fact that the husband had the exclusive opportunity to administer the medicine is relevant as it shows how the crime could have occurred.

Question: Scenario-based: A man joins an illegal gathering while carrying a heavy iron rod. Does this crime allow the police to arrest him without a warrant, and can he be released on bail?

Possible Sections.Section Number: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 144
Possible Sections.Full Text:
(1) In cases where, in the opinion of a District Magistrate, 176[or any other Executive Magistrate] specially empowered by the Government or the District Magistrate to act under this section, there is sufficient ground for proceeding under this section and immediate prevention or speedy remedy is desirable,


    such Magistrate may, by a written order stating the material facts of the case and served in manner provided by section 134, direct any person to abstain from a certain act or to take certain order with certain property in his possession or under his management, if such Magistrate considers that such direction is likely to prevent, or tends to prevent, obstruction, annoyance or injury, or risk or obstruction, annoyance or injury, to any person lawfully employed, or danger to human life, health or safety, or a disturbance of the public tranquillity, or a riot, or an affray.


    (2) An order under this section may, in cases of emergency or in cases where the circumstances do not admit of the serving in due time of a notice upon the person against whom the order is directed, be passed, ex parte.


    (3) An order under this section may be directed to a particular individual, or to the public generally when frequenting or visiting a particular place.


    (4) Any Magistrate may, either on his own motion or on the application of any person aggrieved, rescind or alter any order made under this section by himself or any Magistrate subordinate to him, or by his predecessor in office.


    (5) Where such an application is received, the Magistrate shall afford to the applicant an early opportunity of appearing before him either in person or by pleader and showing cause against the order; and, if the Magistrate rejects the application wholly or in part, he shall record in writing his reasons for so doing.


    (6) No order under this section shall remain in force for more than two months from the making thereof; unless, in cases of danger to human life, health or safety, or a likelihood of a riot or an affray, the Government, by notification in the official Gazette, otherwise directs.


    177[(7) The provisions of this section shall not apply to 178[a Metropolitan Area].]

Relevant Section: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 144

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is an armed unlawful assembly a cognizable and bailable offense under the second schedule?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: Joining an unlawful assembly with a deadly weapon allows for warrantless arrest and is legally classified as a bailable offense.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Since Schedule 2 marks Section 144 as 'May arrest without warrant' and 'Bailable,' the police can arrest the man immediately, but he has the right to bail.
IRAC_Reasoning.Conclusion: Under The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 144, the police can arrest him without a warrant, and he is legally allowed to be released on bail.

Cited Acts and Sections: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, Schedule II, Section 144
source_file: Q-As copy/English/Code of Criminal Procedure/Single hop

Question: Scenario-based: A court is fixing a written contract. Is the judge only allowed to look at what the original words were supposed to be, or can they also look at what the legal result was supposed to be?

Possible Sections.Full Text: In rectifying a written instrument, the Court may inquire what the instrument was intended to mean, and what were intended to be its legal consequences, and is not confined to the inquiry what the language of the instrument was intended to be.

IRAC_Reasoning.Issue: Is a court limited to investigating intended language when rectifying a contract, or can it consider intended legal outcomes?
IRAC_Reasoning.Rule: When fixing a contract, the court can look at both the intended meaning and the intended legal consequences, not just the wording.
IRAC_Reasoning.Application: Under Section 33, the judge has broad power to ensure the contract's legal effect matches what the parties actually planned.
